text.content:
Level: 3
Duration: 1–2 sessions
Location: Hrafnborg outskirts, coast road south/southwest, Singing Islands, Echoing Caves entrance
Key NPCs: Kaelen (traumatized but resolved), Lew (guide), Gragnir (brief, off-scene intelligence)
Type: Travel montage, tactical misdirection, environmental discovery, Grafvitnir Pressure check
Tone: Escape and deception; the weight of responsibility; discovery of the old path

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 25 | Level: 2 | VP into Level: 9

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 25 | Level: 2 | VP into Level: 9

Overview

The party leaves Hrafnborg with the knowledge that Dreyfus is out in the world, that Grafvitnir’s network is hunting them, and that every northeastern road is being watched. They take a calculated risk: flee southwest toward the Singing Islands, the opposite direction from their true goal (Emberwell, far north). The idea is to buy time by misdirecting pursuit while they locate an ancient passage system—underwater caves that connect beneath the landmass and emerge far to the east, near Rindgate.

This beat does three things:

  1. Establishes the escape route and the party’s tactical awareness.

  2. Introduces the Singing Islands geography and the mysterious Echoing Caves.

  3. Reveals that old paths exist below the world, hinting at Yggdrasil’s architecture and the history that Rickety Frets will later explain.

By the end of Beat 10, the party is preparing to descend into the underwater caves. They should feel they’ve bought themselves time—but also be uncertain whether they’ve truly escaped or merely delayed their pursuers.

Part 1: The Departure and the Road South

Scene Setup: Breaking Siege

The party leaves Hrafnborg in the chaos of post-siege recovery. There is no grand sendoff. Harald is occupied with the city’s defense and with managing Kaelen’s condition (post-ritual exhaustion). Gragnir appears briefly—perhaps in a dream, perhaps as a figure glimpsed at the walls—and provides a single piece of intelligence:

⚠ Director Secret: Gragnir’s Message

Gragnir does not give detailed directions. He gives a riddle or a hint:

“The fire that sings is not the fire that burns. Where the earth remembers warmth, the roots remember too. The draugr walk southwest. Walk the opposite shore.”

Or, more direct: “Go south and west. To the coast. Find the singing. The old tunnels are carved into stone older than the kingdoms.”

This gives the party direction but not certainty. They must deduce that:

  1. Singing Islands = Singing (obvious)
  2. “Opposite shore” = where draugr are not (southwest coast, away from interior)
  3. Old tunnels = underwater passages beneath the islands

Gragnir does not travel with them. He remains with Harald, defending the last Ever-Ember in Hrafnborg.

Kaelen’s State

Kaelen emerges from the ritual chamber pale, exhausted, and subtly changed.

  • She has spoken to Yggdrasil. She has felt the tree’s perspective: vast, dying, and not quite comprehending why it is dying. The tree does not blame. It simply fades.

  • She is quiet. She answers questions briefly. She does not volunteer information.

  • If pushed about what she felt in the ritual, she says: “The tree is old. It is tired. It knows what is happening to it. And it… it seemed to recognize me. Like it had been waiting for someone to come.”

  • She understands, at some level, that she is the person it was waiting for—but she does not say this. Not yet.

The Coastal Road

The party travels south/southwest from Hrafnborg along the coast road. The route goes:

  • Hrafnborg → Saltvik: 43 miles, 2–3 days

  • Saltvik → Splinter Fjords: ~30 miles, 1–2 days

  • Splinter Fjords → Singing Islands: ~48 miles, 2–3 days

Total: 120+ miles of coastal travel, rough terrain, 5–8 days depending on conditions.

Distance Management

Rather than play out every day, use 2–3 short scenes along this route:

  1. Saltvik: A brief stop for supplies. The town is crowded with refugees but functional. No immediate Pale Maw interference (they’re still orienting to the party’s southwestern movement).
  2. Splinter Fjords coast: A night’s shelter in a cave or a shepherder’s stone hut. A sign that they’re being followed (Pale Maw scouts on the road). Not a confrontation—just awareness. A Grafvitnir Pressure roll.
  3. Approach to Singing Islands: The landscape becomes volcanic. Steam vents. The air tastes of sulfur. The sound of wind through stone arches grows louder as they approach.

Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Roll 1)

⚠ Grafvitnir Pressure: Road Interference

Make a secret d6 roll. 1–2 = nothing yet; 3–5 = network interference; 6 = major intervention.

Roll Result
1–2 Nothing. The network has not yet oriented to the party’s southwestern route.
3–5 Pale Maw scouts spot them on the coast road. Word spreads via messenger. But the scouts report the party heading southwest, which makes the network think they’re heading for the Singing Islands as a final refuge, not as a waystation on a longer journey. This buys the party time. An NPC in Saltvik or Splinter Fjords might pass along a warning: “There’s word on the road. Cloaked riders asking about a party heading south. They’re saying something about heresy and a stolen woman.”
6 A Pale Maw operative is in Saltvik or Splinter Fjords, ready to intercept. See Grafvitnir Pressure: First Intervention below.

Flavor: Coastal Journey

As the party travels south, the world changes:

  • The towns grow smaller, more isolated, more desperate.

  • The sea is closer. Refugees camp on beaches, hoping for a ship that won’t come.

  • The sky is cloudier; the sun is a pale disc that spends more time below the horizon.

  • The path becomes rockier. Vegetation is minimal—salt-stunted shrubs, and the occasional stunted tree bent by wind.

  • At night, the wind carries the smell of ice and salt. The sound of waves is constant.

This journey should feel like they are moving toward the edge of the world, not deeper into civilization.

Part 2: The Singing Islands Approach

Geography and Atmosphere

The Singing Islands are a chain of volcanic islands off the southwestern coast. The islands are:

  • Geologically active: Hot springs, geothermal vents (the Vent-Gardens on Eldøy), occasional steam explosions.

  • Aurally striking: Winds through stone arches create a constant, eerie singing—not musical, but not random. It shifts with the weather and time of day.

  • Home to Vanir circles: There are settlements here—Eldøy is the largest—but they are not Aesir towns. They are groves, meeting-places, and sacred sites. Different social structures.

  • Partially submerged: The islands have extensive cave systems, many of which are flooded by tidal action or by natural channels that lead underground.

As you approach the Singing Islands, the sound hits you first. It’s not singing—not exactly. It’s the wind moving through stone arches, through caverns, through channels carved by water and time. It rises and falls, almost like breath. Almost like something vast is singing a note it doesn’t know it’s making.

The islands emerge from the gray water like the ribs of something long dead, picked clean by the sea. Volcanic stone, dark and jagged. Steam rises from cracks in the earth. There are no trees, but there are structures—standing stones, carved pillars, some so old that ice has begun to smooth their edges.

The largest island, Eldøy, shows smoke rising from its summit. That’s where the geothermal vents feed the Vent-Gardens, where the only vegetables in Svellheim still grow.

But you’re not here for Eldøy. You’re here for the caves.

Discovering the Echoing Caves

The Echoing Caves are to the west of the Singing Islands proper. They are not immediately obvious—they are partially submerged, accessible only at certain tides, and their entrance is hidden behind a rocky outcrop.

The party needs to either:

  1. Ask around (in Eldøy or a fishing settlement) about old routes and cave systems. A Vanir elder or a fisher might mention the caves and the fact that they “go down deep, further than should be possible.”

  2. Stumble upon them while exploring the coastline.

  3. Use Gragnir’s hint (if they remember it: “the old tunnels are carved into stone older than the kingdoms”) to deduce that the Echoing Caves are likely candidates.

A successful Reason test (moderate difficulty, or Intuition if they’re using a skill like Lore or Navigation) lets them deduce that the caves are large, that they were historically important, and that they may connect other islands or regions underground.

Environmental Discovery: The Underwater Passage

The Echoing Caves entrance is a large, arched mouth of dark stone. At high tide, water fills the lower portion, obscuring the passage. At low tide, there is a path—a few feet of stone beach before the water takes over.

The party will need to do some scouting:

  • A Presence test (checking for spirits, wards, or signs of habitation) reveals no current occupants, but there are old carvings—Vanir runes, some so weathered they are barely legible. They speak of “the Deep Road” and “the Root Ways.”

  • The passage descends. It is walkable for the first 50 feet, then it transitions to shallow water (waist-deep in places, chest-deep in others). The water is cold, clear, and perfectly still.

  • If they wade in, they will see that the water continues onward—a flooded tunnel. The passage is wide enough for two people to walk side-by-side. The ceiling is high enough that there is air for breathing. But the path forward is entirely underwater—or appears to be, until closer inspection reveals an air pocket every 30–40 feet, where the tunnel curves and the stone rises above the water line.

This is ancient. Yggdrasil’s roots don’t create caves, but the architects of the First Kindling knew how to use them.

Montage: Cave Entrance and Preparation

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success / 0 VP on failure (2 rounds)

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success / 0 VP on failure (2 rounds)

Run a Moderate Montage Test representing the preparation and initial descent into the underwater caves.

Difficulty: Moderate

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Moderate (adjusted for 4 heroes) 4 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds (each hero acts once per round; 8 total individual tests).

Round 1 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Scout the entrance and tidal rhythm Intuition or Reason Study the cave mouth, understand how the tides move through it. Find supplies already here—old rope, broken tools, fragments of past travelers’ gear. Mark safe entry points.
Prepare gear for water travel Might or Agility Secure packs, test rope for strength, distribute weight so everything stays buoyant. Identify what can stay dry and what must be abandoned.
Calm and ready the group Presence Speak to fears about the underwater passage. Ground the party in resolve. Someone is afraid; talk them through it.

Round 1 Interlude: The First Waters

The cave entrance opens like a mouth, dark and wet. The water inside is so clear you can see the stone beneath—carved deliberately, not by erosion. Someone shaped this passage long ago.

As you wade in, the temperature drops. The water is cold, shockingly so. But it’s not numbing. It’s alive. You can feel the current, faint and patient, pulling toward the deeper places.

The bioluminescent creatures drift past your feet, glowing faintly blue. They’re moving with purpose, deeper in. Following the dark.

The party has crossed the threshold. There is no turning back now. The water grows deeper with each step.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Wade through the first flooded sections Might or Agility Move through waist-deep water over treacherous stone. Keep footing, manage cold shock. The passage is slippery and the current tugs at your legs.
Navigate the first air pocket Reason or Intuition Find the chamber where stone rises above water. Reach it without panic. There are side passages here; one leads deeper (correct), one leads to a dead-end (wrong).
Keep spirits up in the darkness Presence The lack of external light is disorienting. Encourage the party. Ration torches wisely. Keep hope alive.

Round 2 Interlude: The Stone Remembers

In the air pocket, you emerge gasping. Your breath comes in heavy clouds. The cold has numbed your feet.

On the wall above you, carvings gleam in torchlight. Not human work. The lines are too precise, too deliberate. They spiral in patterns that almost hurt to look at—not painful, but wrong, as if your eyes are trying to interpret something that doesn’t exist in normal geometry.

One carving stands out: a figure with water flowing from uplifted hands. Below it, in a language that predates the kingdoms, a single word is cut deep into stone:

Líf.

The Mother. The one who survived the ending. The one who brought renewal.

You are not the first to seek renewal. You are not the first to walk these old roads.

Kaelen traces the carving, understanding more than she reveals.

Final Interlude: The Deep Song

By the time you reach your camp for the night, the sound of the caves has changed. It’s not silence anymore. It’s a sound—so faint you’re not sure if you’re hearing it or feeling it—like the stone itself is singing.

You are deeper now than any surface dweller is meant to go. The old roads are old for a reason. They remember times before kingdoms. Times before the Keepers.

And something in the darkness remembers you.

The party is committed now. There is no return. Only forward, into the deep.

Montage Outcomes

Result Outcome
Hard Success (all successes) The party navigates smoothly through the initial passages. They find supplies: old rope, torches, preserved rations. They reach the first major air pocket in good condition, rested and ready to move deeper.
Success (more successes than failures) They make adequate progress with minor difficulties. The cold is manageable. They reach camp tired but unharmed. One supply is damaged (rope frays, some torches waterlogged).
Failure (more failures than successes) They are soaked, exhausted, and struggling with the passage. Equipment is damaged. Heat expenditure is high. Cold is a persistent problem. They camp in a less-safe location, unsure of the way forward.
Hard Failure (all failures) Panic in the water. Someone nearly drowns and requires rescue. Supplies are largely ruined. Heat cost is extreme. They camp in fear, aware they may have made a terrible mistake.

The Passage Itself (Scene)

The tunnel is ancient. The walls are smooth—not from water alone, but from deliberate shaping. You can feel tool marks beneath your fingertips where the stone has been carved into channels. Are they decorative? Functional? It’s hard to tell.

The water is so clear that you can see 20 feet ahead. Small bioluminescent things—crustaceans, or something older—glow faintly in the darkness. They cluster in patches, following some logic only they understand.

Every 30 or 40 feet, the tunnel rises. There’s an air pocket where the stone ceiling breaks above the waterline. You can climb out, breathe, rest for a moment. But the water always returns. The tunnel always sinks again.

In one pocket, you notice something that makes Kaelen go very still: a carved figure on the wall. A woman, or something shaped like one, with water flowing from her hands. Below her, in a language that might be older than writing, a name:

Líf.

Kaelen recognizes it. It is the name of the woman in the old stories—the one who survived Ragnarök. The one who would repopulate the world when it woke again.

“She was an Ember-Keeper,” Kaelen whispers. “Or… the Keepers were named after her. I’m not sure anymore which came first.”

This is a moment of lore discovery and character revelation. Kaelen is beginning to understand that the path beneath the world is much older than she thought. The Keepers did not invent these routes. They inherited them.

Part 3: The Warden’s Corruption

Discovery

The party arrives at the Vent-Gardens of Eldøy in late afternoon. The gardens occupy a terraced slope on the island’s western face, where geothermal heat rises from deep vents and keeps a roughly circular radius warm enough for plants to grow. It is one of the only places in Svellheim where food still grows.

It is also wrong.

The gardens should smell of earth and growing things. Instead, the air tastes of metal and ash. The plants—what remains of them—are blackening from the roots outward, as if something is poisoning them from beneath the stone itself.

The terraces are cracked. One has collapsed entirely, a section of cultivation stone tumbling downward in a half-buried ruin. The irrigation channels, which should run with clean mineral water, are sluggish. What flows is dark, viscous, and when you touch it, it leaves a residue on your skin—thin, oily, faintly warm even in the cold air. It smells wrong: not quite flesh, not quite stone, but something that has decayed beyond either state.

An islander is weeping beside the collapsed terrace. An older woman, her hands muddy. When she sees you, she does not move—too exhausted, or too grief-stricken to care who approaches.

“They’re gone,” she says, not looking at you. “All of them. The vents died. The Warden killed them.”

Investigation

Investigation reveals the tragedy:

  • A Reason test (moderate difficulty, TN 14) uncovers what has happened. The geothermal vents that powered the gardens have become unstable—some blocked by something seeping up from below, others producing steam that is tainted with a dark, necrotic substance. The islanders have been unable to tend the plants for two weeks. Everything is dying.

  • An Intuition test (moderate, TN 14) to speak with the grieving woman—named Sigrid—gets her to explain: The Vent-Warden is an ancient spirit that has guarded these gardens for centuries, perhaps longer. It was kind to the islanders, protective. Two weeks ago, it changed. It withdrew into the vents. It stopped emerging to tend them. Now the vents are poisoned. The plants are failing.

  • A Nature or Lore test (hard, TN 16) by Kaelen or someone with arcane knowledge reveals something Sigrid could not articulate: This is not the Warden’s doing—this is something infecting the Warden. The pattern of corruption, the dark seepage, the withdrawal from its duties—these are symptoms of the Rot. The spirit is being consumed from within.

📝 GM Notes: Reading the Signs

If Kaelen makes the Nature test, she goes quiet and her hand moves to the ever-ember mark on her chest. She has felt the Rot before. She knows what corruption looks like from the inside.

If Lew is present and makes the test (Intuition), his eye pulses with that strange warmth. He looks at the dark water and says softly: “This is the same poison. The same taste. It’s here too. It’s everywhere now.”

The party may recognize that the mercy option exists at this point—a perceptive or experienced player will realize something is being lost, not just destroyed—but they should not feel guided toward it. That comes later.

The Warden

The Vent-Warden is known to the islanders by that name alone. No one remembers its true name, if they ever knew it. Jarðvættr is an old word, old enough that most Svellheimers would not recognize it: Earth-Wight, keeper of the ground, spirit of place.

It has been here since long before the towns were built. Sigrid’s great-grandmother’s stories mention it. The Vanir elders know it as one of the old ones—not human, not quite a god, but a force of nature given something like consciousness. It shaped the heat of the vents, guided the mineral flow, ensured the gardens grew. It was territorial but not malicious. It would not allow the islanders to damage the vents or the gardens, but it did not prevent them from tending the growth.

It was, perhaps, happy in its way. Tending was its purpose.

Two weeks ago, something else began tending the vents instead.

The Warden emerges from the largest vent—the central one, around which the highest terraces were built—and for a moment you cannot understand what you are looking at.

It is shaped like nothing in the natural world. Large, perhaps twice the height of a man, with a form that shifts between solid and liquid. Its body is volcanic stone, rough and dark, but cracks run across it—not fractures, but channels. Through those channels, something black flows. When the creature moves, the stone groans.

Its face is the worst part. It has a face—a mouth, eyes, the suggestion of a nose—but they are wrong. The mouth opens too wide. The eyes are pits of black ichor that seems to move independently of the skull. And yet, beneath the wrongness, you sense something else: an intelligence that is being ground beneath the corruption. A will that is not its own.

The creature makes a sound. Not a roar. It is almost like a name—a name in a language older than words, a sound that tries to be speech and fails. Then it fixes its corrupted gaze on you.

“The heat… dies,” it says, and the voice is two things at once: the grinding of stone and the wet whisper of rot. “Everything… dies.”

Combat: The Vent-Gardens

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Boss) | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 1 VP on full victory / 2 VP on mercy victory

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Boss) | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 1 VP on full victory / 2 VP on mercy victory

⚔️ Combat Setup: The Warden’s Corruption

Difficulty: Hard (Level 2 PCs, partyES 136, EV 148)

Enemy Count: 1 Jarðvættr (Level 5 Brute Elite, EV 28), 6x Rot-Warped Landvaettr (EV 60), 3x Oath-Broken Draugr (EV 60). Total EV: 148. Phase 2 (below 60 Stamina): Rot-Echoes spawn each round.

Terrain:

  • Stone cultivation terraces: 1 square high. Movement costs 2 squares to climb; provides half-cover when crouching behind one.
  • Irrigation channels: 1-square-wide trenches of mineral-hot water between terraces. Crossing costs 1 extra movement; falling in causes 3 fire damage and the creature is slowed until it spends a move action to climb out.
  • Collapsed wall sections and scattered debris: difficult terrain.
  • 4 active steam vents (place on map): each occupies 1 square, produces heavy steam (obscures line of sight through the vent square). In Phase 1, standing adjacent to a vent is safe. In Phase 2, standing adjacent to a vent at the start of your turn costs 3 fire damage.

Victory Conditions:

  • Combat Victory: Reduce Jarðvættr to 0 Stamina. It collapses into stone and stops moving. The Rot dissipates.
  • Mercy Victory (optional): Achieve 3 successful Unwilling Vessel attempts (see “The Mercy Option” below). On the third success, the corruption tears free and dissipates. The Jarðvættr collapses, inert but alive.

Lighting: The steam vents provide dim light. The area is shadowy but not dark.

Special Rules:

  • Vents as Movement: The Jarðvættr can use its Vent-Step maneuver to move between vents (see stat block).
  • Heat Hazard (Phase 2 only): Standing adjacent to a vent at the start of a creature’s turn costs 3 fire damage. The corrupted vents are now dangerous.
  • Mercy Condition: If the party is attempting a mercy victory, track Mercy Counter. Each successful T3 result on an Unwilling Vessel attempt advances it by 1. At 3, the encounter ends with the Warden freed.

Stat Blocks

Rot-Echoes (Phase 2 spawns):

  • @UUID[Actor.8G3EVP3A9Lze74D7]{Rot-Echo} (Level 2 Minion, 8 SP)

Note: 1 Rot-Echo spawns adjacent to the Jarðvættr at the start of each of its turns once it drops to 36 SP or below.

⚠ Entity Gap: Jarðvættr

No Svellheim Entities module entry exists for the Jarðvættr. Use the stat block below until an entity is created.

Jarðvættr — The Vent-Warden (Level 5 Brute Elite)

Spirit, Elemental, Corrupted | Large (2×2) | Keywords: Spirit, Elemental, Corrupted

Stamina: 72  |  Speed: 5  |  Size: L  |  Stability: 3

Characteristics: Might +5, Agility +1, Reason −1, Intuition +3, Presence +2

Defenses: Might 16 | Agility 13 | Reason 11 | Intuition 14 | Presence 13

Main Actions (2 per round)

Scorching Grasp (Melee, Might): +6 vs. Might; 11 fire damage. Target is Burning (4 fire damage at start of each turn, save ends TN 12). The Jarðvættr can only have one target Burning at a time. Applying Burning to a new target ends it on the previous target.

Vent-Blast (Ranged Area: 2×3 line originating from any active steam vent, Might): +5 vs. Agility; 8 fire damage and target is knocked prone. Creatures that fail by 5 or more are also pushed 2 squares away from the vent. Costs 1 Malice.

Heaving Slam (Melee, Might; Phase 2 only, unlocks at ≤36 Stamina): +6 vs. Might; 14 damage (7 fire + 7 necrotic). Target is dazed (save ends TN 14). The ground within 1 square of the target becomes difficult terrain as Rot-ichor seeps upward.

Maneuvers

Vent-Step (Maneuver): The Jarðvættr submerges into any active steam vent and emerges from any other active vent as part of the same maneuver. While submerged, it cannot be targeted or harmed. Unlimited range within the encounter area.

Features

Steam Shroud: While within 1 square of an active steam vent, the Jarðvættr has concealment. Attacks against it have a bane.

Volcanic Resilience: Immune to fire damage and resistant to all damage (reduce incoming damage by 3 before applying).

Rot Corruption (Phase 2): When the Jarðvættr drops to 36 Stamina or below, the Rot takes fuller control. At the start of each of its turns, 1 Rot-Echo spawns in an adjacent square. Vent-Step now costs nothing (can be used before other actions). Steam vents emit black-tinged smoke.

Unwilling Vessel: A hero adjacent to the Jarðvættr can spend their Main Action to attempt to reach the spirit inside (Nature, Lore, or Presence; Hard difficulty, TN 16). On a T3 power roll (17+), the Jarðvættr does not attack that hero this round and the Mercy Counter advances by 1. On a T2 roll (12–16), the Jarðvættr hesitates and loses its next maneuver. On a T1 roll (≤11), no effect.

Tragic Memory: The first time the Jarðvættr drops below 36 Stamina, read aloud the Phase 2 interlude text (see below).

Malice Abilities

Geyser Eruption (3 Malice): One active vent erupts violently. All creatures within 2 squares make a Hard Agility test (TN 16). On failure, 14 fire damage; on success, 7 fire damage. The vent is inactive for the rest of the encounter.

Choking Steam (2 Malice): All heroes in the encounter area are weakened (−2 to all power rolls) until the start of the Director’s next turn. Heroes adjacent to a steam vent are weakened until the end of their next turn (save ends TN 12) instead.

Rot Surge (5 Malice; Phase 2 only): All creatures in the encounter area take 5 necrotic damage. Every active steam vent emits Rot-ichor; any hero who starts their next turn adjacent to a vent takes an additional 3 necrotic damage.

Phase 1 (72–37 Stamina)

The creature emerges, and the ground beneath it trembles. Steam rises in a choking column. The air itself becomes hostile—the heat warps vision, makes breathing difficult. The Warden’s corrupted form shifts and flows. Black veins run through its stone body, spreading and retracting like a heartbeat that is no longer its own.

“Burn,” it whispers, and flame erupts from the nearest vent. “Everything must burn.” But beneath the words, you hear something else: anguish. A consciousness still present enough to despair at what it has become.

📝 Running Phase 1

The encounter is a fire and positioning puzzle. The Jarðvættr uses the vents for mobility and concealment. Heroes must either hunt it through the terrain or burn through its high fire resistance and damage reduction. The Scorching Grasp forces target management (only one Burning target at a time; switching targets ends the previous condition). This phase should feel like fighting a primal force—dangerous, but still comprehensible.

Phase 2 (≤36 Stamina)

When the Jarðvættr drops below 36 Stamina, read:

A sound rises from the creature—not a roar, but something almost like a name. An old name, in a language that predates the kingdoms. The smoke changes colour for just a moment: clear and warm, the way steam should be. The gardens remember what they once were.

Then the black floods back.

The Rot surges through the creature’s form, and Rot-Echoes begin to manifest around it—shapes of shadow and corrupted water, spawning from the poisoned vents. The Warden is no longer fighting. It is being fought through. Its body is becoming a gateway.

📝 Running Phase 2

Phase 2 is horror meeting sacrifice. The Warden fights harder (Heaving Slam unlocks, phase-shifted movement), but it is no longer the primary threat. The spawning Rot-Echoes are—they multiply each round. The encounter shifts from “defeat the creature” to “save the creature” (for those attempting mercy) or “end its suffering before it becomes a vector for the Rot entirely.”

Director’s Malice Track: The Director starts with 2 Malice per round (standard).

Key Decisions for the GM:

  • Encouraging Recognition: If no hero has made the Nature or Lore test yet, and they’re not showing signs of understanding the Rot corruption, drop a hint in combat. Perhaps Kaelen’s ember-mark pulses when the creature howls. Perhaps Lew’s eye reacts to the black seepage. The mercy option should be discoverable, not hidden—but also not obvious.
  • Mercy Tracking: If heroes are attempting Unwilling Vessel rolls, keep a visible tally of Mercy Counter (0/3, 1/3, etc.). This gives them feedback that their approach is working without betraying the mechanics.
  • Pacing Phase 2: Don’t let Phase 2 drag. Rot-Echoes multiply quickly. After 2–3 rounds of Phase 2, the encounter should feel urgent—either finish the killing blow, or commit fully to the mercy path.
  • Ending Mercy: When the third Unwilling Vessel T3 success occurs, don’t play it as anti-climactic. The Rot corruption visibly tears itself free—describe black ichor fountaining upward, the Warden’s form stabilizing and solidifying, its eyes clearing for just a moment. It collapses, exhausted, no longer hosting the parasite. The Rot dissipates like smoke. The encounter ends.

The Mercy Option

If the party recognises the Jarðvættr as a corrupted spirit (a Reason test TN 14 during the first round of combat, or automatically if Kaelen succeeds on a Nature test TN 12 at any point), they can pursue an alternative to killing the creature.

The Unwilling Vessel feature allows heroes to attempt to reach the spirit beneath the Rot. Each successful T3 roll (17+) advances a Mercy Counter. When the counter reaches 3, the Rot corruption tears itself free of the spirit—a visible, cathartic moment—and the Jarðvættr collapses, inert but alive and cleansed.

Telegraphing Mercy (Without Forcing It)

  • After the first round of combat, if no hero has attempted Unwilling Vessel, have an islander (perhaps Sigrid, who followed the party out of desperation) shout from the safety of the terraces: “Don’t kill it! Please! The Warden—it didn’t choose this! It’s trapped!” This gives narrative permission to try mercy without making it obligatory.

  • If Kaelen recognised the Rot, she might say quietly during the fight: “This isn’t what it is. This is what was done to it. There might be another way.” This is a character-driven suggestion, not a mechanical push.

  • Each successful Unwilling Vessel attempt should be described narratively. Describe a moment of clarity in the creature’s eyes. A flash of the garden as it once was. A memory of growth instead of decay. These descriptions reinforce that mercy is possible.

Combat During Mercy Attempt

The Jarðvættr does not get a free pass. Even while the party is attempting mercy, the creature still acts—it still uses Malice abilities, spawns Rot-Echoes in Phase 2, and threatens the party. Pursuing mercy is harder, not easier, because heroes are spending Main Actions on Unwilling Vessel instead of attacking.

This creates genuine tension: Do you damage the creature further, or risk the long mercy path while it continues to hurt you?

Rewards and Consequences

⚔️ Kill Path

  • 1 Victory (for defeating the Jardvaettr (Level 5 Brute Elite)).
  • Supplies: The islanders provide food, rope, and materials for the descent into the caves (standard amount).
  • Word Economy: −1 reputation with Vanir communities. The islanders mourn the Warden. Even though it was corrupted, they grieve its loss. They knew it for generations. A simple “it was infected” does not ease that grief.

🌿 Mercy Path

  • 2 Victories (for saving a corrupted spirit rather than destroying it).
  • Supplies: Double the standard amount. The islanders are profoundly grateful—this was not just a victory, but a redemption.
  • Word Economy: +1 reputation with Vanir communities. The islanders mark the party as friends of the Singing Islands. Stories of the deed will travel with them northward.
  • Narrative Consequence: The Jarðvættr does not join the party. It collapses into a deep, healing rest within the largest vent. But before it closes itself away, it offers a gift—a fragment of its own essence, imprinted with knowledge of the Singing Islands and the waters beneath them.
  • Reward: @UUID[Item.3oc5b0bAOIjgSk2A]{Jarðvættr’s Gift} — Any hero who touches the fallen creature gains an edge on Intuition or Reason tests related to navigation, ancient places, or the deep paths for the rest of the journey northward.

Both Paths

The party leaves the Singing Islands knowing that the Rot is not just a northern problem. It has reached this far, into places as old as time. The gardens are not saved—the Warden’s corruption has poisoned the vents beyond simple healing. But there is hope: if a corrupted spirit can be cleansed, perhaps the Rot has limits. Perhaps it can be fought, not just endured.

Sigrid—or another islander—gives the party a final gift: knowledge of the Echoing Caves’ exit point. “The caves go northeast, under the world. The old maps say they emerge near Rindgate, on the far side of the inland passes. It’s a long journey through dark water. But you will come out where your hunters won’t expect.”

Pacing Note

This beat is a gift before the darkness of the caves. The party has had a moment of genuine choice—to fight or to save. The encounter is difficult (a Hard solo boss), but it is not impossible. The terrain is complex but navigable. Most importantly, it is warm.

Let the party take their time in the Vent-Gardens. Describe the heat, the residual memory of growing things, the sound of water that is not quite dead. If they choose mercy, celebrate that choice. If they choose to kill, do not punish them—but let the islanders’ grief hang in the air.

Either way, they have glimpsed something of Yggdrasil’s ancient past. That knowledge will matter in the darkness ahead.

Part 4: The Decision to Descend

By the end of this beat, the party has discovered the flooded cave passage. It is passable, but it requires commitment: a 3–4 day journey through water, dependent on air pockets and the maintenance of Light sources and Heat.

The question before them is simple: Do they go down into the dark, or do they continue overland and risk the open roads where Grafvitnir’s network is hunting them?

For maximum dramatic tension:

  • Have them camp at one of the air pockets and discuss options.

  • One party member might argue for the caves (it’s safer, faster if it works).

  • Another might argue for caution (unknown passage, risk of drowning, what if the caves lead nowhere?).

  • Kaelen might say: “The old roads are still roads. Someone kept them passable. We should trust that.”

After they decide (and they will decide to go down), Gragnir’s voice might echo in their memory, or Kaelen might recite a fragment of the old Keeper liturgy:

“Where the roots remember the sun, the way is open to those who remember the roots.”

The party descends into the darkness.

NPCs: Locals on the Coast

👤 Lysandre the Fishwife

A Vanir elder who remembers the Singing Islands before Fimbulwinter. She can describe the Echoing Caves as “the old road, the deep road, the way the First Keepers walked before there were roads above.” She won’t go down herself, but she can offer supplies and stories. She might exact a small price: a promise to remember her if the party survives the north.

👤 Riku the Explorer

A younger person obsessed with old maps and routes. He has a fragment of a map showing the underwater passage and the direction it leads (northeast). He wants to go with the party, but he’s a liability in combat. The party can leave him behind or bring him (which costs them time and Heat).

If the party takes Riku, he becomes a minor NPC who might provide useful information later, but he is also Grafvitnir Pressure waiting to happen—if captured, he knows everything about the underwater route and can betray them.

Grafvitnir Pressure: First Intervention

If the d6 roll in Part 1 indicated major intervention (roll of 6), then a Pale Maw operative is waiting in Eldøy or the Singing Islands.

📝 Negotiation Option

The operative is a true believer, not a mercenary. They believe the party is heretics protecting a false Keeper. If the party can convince them that Kaelen is actually trying to end the world (a lie, but thematically rich), the operative might back down. Very Hard negotiation.

GM Notes

📝 Director Guidance

  • Pacing: This beat can be one session if the party moves quickly, or two sessions if they linger in Eldøy or Splinter Fjords for investigation and supply management.

Overview

The party descends into the flooded caves that stretch beneath the landmass. What they discover is that the caves are no simple natural formation—they are ancient, deliberately carved, and they carry the marks of Yggdrasil's roots and the civilization that served it.

More disturbing: the caves are occupied. Not by Vanir spirits or peaceful landvaettir, but by undead, moving in organized columns, working with purpose, heading east toward the interior and beyond. This is the first concrete sign that the dead are not simply wandering or driven by hunger. Something is directing them.

Kaelen recognizes some of the carvings and symbols—fragments of old Keeper knowledge. This frightens her more than the undead do.

By the end of this beat, the party understands:

  1. There are old roads beneath the world.
  2. The dead are using them.
  3. The Rot is visible here—spreading from deep roots, poisoning the stone itself.
  4. The Keepers were not the first to use these caves. They inherited them.

Part 1: The Deep Descent

Days 1–2: The Routine of Passage

The first two days in the caves are almost mundane. The party walks and wades through a flooded tunnel. The bioluminescent creatures provide faint, eerie light. They rest in air pockets, eat cold rations, manage their Heat carefully.

Lew is steady. He has guided people through worse places (in his own mind, probably exaggerating). He keeps everyone focused on the practical: distance covered, supplies consumed, next rest point.

Kaelen is quiet but observant. She traces her fingers along carved symbols on the walls, some of which she recognizes. She doesn't share what they mean. Not yet. They make her more pale.

Establish routine and rhythm:

  • Each day's journey is 8–12 hours of walking/wading, depending on conditions.
  • Each night they camp in an air pocket. Heat is consumed for warmth (the caves are cold—probably 40–50 degrees Fahrenheit). They use torches for light. Water is available but cold.
  • The bioluminescence provides constant, faint, alien light—never quite bright enough to see clearly, making everything seem slightly dreamlike.

Round 1 Interlude: The Carved Passage

The tunnel branches, and you choose. The correct path opens into something vast—a chamber easily 100 feet across. The ceiling rises into shadow, perhaps 40 feet high. Every inch of stone is carved.

Not random. Not decay. Deliberate.

Spirals intertwine. Geometric patterns repeat. Figures humanoid and animal, symbols that look almost like writing in a language older than kingdoms. And the bioluminescence is dense here, clustered in thick patches that glow blue and green and sickly yellow, following the carved lines as if reading a map written for eyes that see in darkness.

The water here is still. It's a cathedral made by something that died long ago.

And on the wall, clear as morning: a figure with water flowing from uplifted hands. Below it, in a language Kaelen recognizes but does not know:

Líf.

The Mother. The one who survived the ending. The one who brought renewal.

Kaelen recognizes the Root-Runes. She knows now that the Keepers were not the first. Someone older came before, carved these paths, left these marks. The weight of that knowledge settles on her shoulders.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Manage deepening cold and exposure Might or Presence The water grows colder as they descend. Torches burn faster. The air in the passages is thin, difficult to breathe. Keep the group from succumbing to hypothermia and despair.
Navigate the changing cave system Reason or Agility The passages change character. Some sections are tight, forcing them to squeeze through. Others open wide. Find the main tunnel among the side chambers. Keep direction when landmarks blur together.
Sense disturbances in the water and air Intuition or Presence Something feels wrong. The water currents are unusual. There are sounds from deeper down—not echoes, but movement. Is something else in these caves?

Round 2 Interlude: The Silence and the Weight

By the end of Day 2, the cold has seeped into your bones. You are soaked despite efforts to stay dry. Your breath clouds in the weak light of the bioluminescence. The torches sputter and burn faster, consuming themselves in the thin air.

The silence in these caves is profound. No wind, no bird song, no distant water. Just the sound of your own breathing and the slow drip of water somewhere in the darkness.

And then, something changes. The water grows warmer—not warm, but less freezing. The creatures shift their patterns, suddenly moving faster, flowing toward deeper passages like water going down a drain.

Kaelen watches them go, her face pale.

"Something is calling them," she whispers. "Something deeper. Something that the Keepers wrote about but did not describe."

The caves remember a purpose older than the Keepers. That purpose still echoes.

The party is deep now. Committed. The boundary between the known world and the ancient deep is blurring.

Post-Montage Interlude: The Organized Dead

On Day 3, exhaustion takes hold. Your thoughts move slowly. The cold numbs even thought.

Then, ahead, movement.

Not the bioluminescent creatures. Something larger. Shapes in the water, moving with purpose. They emerge from the darkness moving in columns—slow, deliberate, organized.

Draugr. Perhaps 30 or 40 of them. Moving east-southeast. Some carry tools. Some carry bundles. All move in perfect silence, as if they are following a path carved into their rotting minds.

The party manages to conceal themselves in a side passage. The draugr column passes, unseeing, unhearing. They march with direction and will. Something is orchestrating them. Something with intelligence, with purpose.

When they are gone, the caves are silent again. But the silence is no longer empty. It is full—pregnant with intention.

The old roads are being used. Someone is building something in the deep.

The party comprehends now: the dead are not random. The dead are organized. This is not a natural catastrophe. This is being directed by something intelligent, something aware, something that dwells in the deepest places.

Hard Success (all successes) The party completes the three days smoothly and safely. They navigate well, manage supplies efficiently, avoid the worst of the cold, and reach the next major chamber in good condition. They gather supplies: preserved rations, dried fish, alchemical vials. They understand that the Keepers' knowledge still shapes these caves.
Success (more successes than failures) They complete the three days with minor complications. One complication (difficult passage, unusual cold, or unsettling encounter) costs them supplies or time, but nothing serious. They reach camp tired but functional.

Part 2: The Bioluminescent Grotto

Scene: Wonder and Discovery

On Day 3 or 4, the tunnel opens into a vast chamber. This is not a natural cave—the ceiling is too regular, the walls too smooth, and there is clearly a pattern to the carvings that cover every surface.

The chamber takes your breath away.

It is easily 100 feet across and twice that in length. The ceiling is lost in shadow, but it's high—probably 40 feet or more. And the walls...

Every inch of stone is carved. Not haphazardly, but with deliberation. There are spirals that intertwine, geometric patterns that repeat, and figures—humanoid shapes, animal shapes, symbols that look almost like writing but in a language older than any you know.

And the light. The bioluminescence is intense here, almost blinding after days in the dim passages. The creatures have clustered on the walls in thick patches. They glow in shades of blue, green, and sickly yellow. They follow patterns carved into the stone, as if they're reading a map written for eyes that see in darkness.

The water here is still. It's like walking into a cathedral made by something that died long ago.

Kaelen stops at the entrance. She doesn't move. Her hand is on the stone wall, tracing one of the carvings. When she speaks, her voice is barely a whisper:

"These are Root-Runes. Old Keeper marking. But I… I've never seen this version. This is older. This is the version that the Keepers learned from, not the other way around."

She looks at you, and there's fear in her eyes.

"Someone was here before the Keepers. Someone taught us how to read the roots."

Investigation: The Chamber's Purpose

A successful Reason test (moderate difficulty) or a character with Lore skill can determine several things about the chamber:

  1. It was a library or archive. The carvings are too organized to be decoration. They follow thematic groupings—sections of wall seem to have different "topics."
  2. It was maintained. The carvings are clear, sharp. They haven't been worn away or damaged by time. Someone kept this place.
  3. It is/was sacred. The geometry is too precise, the symbols too particular. This was a place of ritual significance.
  4. It might still be in use. The bioluminescent creatures aren't randomly placed. They follow the carved paths. Is this intentional? Are they being "guided" to specific locations?

Kaelen's Recognition

Kaelen recognizes the Root-Runes because she was trained in them. She spent years in the Keeper archives learning the language of Yggdrasil. But she has never seen them in this form.

The oldest Keeper texts spoke of "learning the Root-Tongue from the first writings." She always assumed this was metaphorical—a poetic way of saying the Keepers studied nature. She now realizes it was literal. The Keepers found written knowledge already here, already carved, and they learned a language that was not invented by humans.

If pressed, she admits: "The Keepers were not the originators. We were… inheritors. Guardians of something we only half-understood."

This should shake the party. It raises the question: Who carved these runes? Who were the "first writers"?

The answer (hinted at, not stated): These were carved by something that came before the current age. Possibly by Yggdrasil itself, in a form that could interact with the physical world. Or by the Vanir, or another civilization. The truth is lost.

The Unsettling Detail: The Bioluminescence Pattern

Have a character with high Intuition or Perception make a Presence test (hard difficulty).

If they succeed: They notice that the bioluminescent creatures are not randomly arranged. They follow the carved paths on the walls. More: they seem to be flowing in a specific direction—following a pattern that spirals deeper into the chamber, toward a specific wall section.

What are they following? It's not clear. But it suggests intelligence, or at minimum, a response to something.

⚠ GM Secret: Bioluminescence and the Rot

The creatures are being drawn by the Rot. The Rot spreads through the Root-Ways like a disease, and these creatures are attracted to it, or are being manipulated by it. This is the first sign that the Rot has a presence even this deep in the world.

Part 3: The First Encounter — Undead Wolf Pack

Transition: The Ritual Interruption

As the party is exploring or resting in the grotto, something disturbs the water.

Low in the chamber, at the far end, the water begins to move. It's not a current. It's something beneath the surface, moving with purpose.

A Presence test gives warning. Otherwise, it's a surprise.

The undead wolf pack emerges. These are not normal draugr. They are corpses of ancient sea-wolves—creatures that drowned when the caves flooded, centuries ago. They have risen as undead, but their state is worse than human draugr. Their fur is matted with slime and ice. Their eyes are white and sightless. They are blind, and therefore they hunt by sound and smell.

The party's noise in the grotto has drawn them.

Part 4: The Draugr Column

Transition: Resting After Combat

After defeating the wolves, the party will want to rest, heal, and recover. Let them. They've earned it.

They rest in the grotto for several hours (or a full night if needed). Kaelen tends wounds. Lew scouts for better water or additional supplies.

The Observation

As the party is resting or preparing to move deeper, they hear something that freezes their blood: marching. Not the sound of animals or chaos, but of organized, rhythmic movement.

Footsteps. Slow. Regular. Like a column of soldiers, or an army of the dead, moving with purpose.

A Presence test (moderate difficulty) allows the party to hear it coming from deeper in the caves, moving from east to west. The sound grows louder, then fades.

The party has three options:

  1. Hide and observe. The party can conceal themselves in the grotto's side passages and watch the column pass. This is Safe but requires good stealth checks.
  2. Investigate and follow. The party can shadow the column, trying to understand what it is.
  3. Confront. The party can step out and demand explanation (unwise, but possible).

The Column: What They See

If the party successfully hides and observes, read:

They emerge from the deeper passages like a flood, but silent. Draugr. Maybe 30 or 40 of them, moving in formation. Not chaos. Not the random wandering you've seen on the surface.

They march in columns of four or five. They carry tools—picks, spades, ropes. Some carry crude weapons. Some carry bundles wrapped in cloth. They move with direction.

And they're not shambling or groaning. There's no sound except the sound of their feet on stone and the occasional metallic clink of a tool.

More: they move as if following a path. Their footsteps are exact, precise. They're not searching. They know where they're going.

The undead in the column are varied. Some are fresh—corpses from the last few months. Some are ancient—so degraded that it's amazing they still move. All of them are moving with the same purpose.

A few are clearly larger, stronger. Directing the column with gestures and guttural sounds that might once have been speech.

And as the column passes, Kaelen grabs your arm. Her hand is shaking.

"They're organized. The dead are… they're being directed. This isn't Níðhöggr's hunger. This is someone. Something. Someone is building an army."

Kaelen's Recognition

⚠ Director Secret: The Keeper Binding

This moment is crucial for Kaelen's character. She has theorized about the undead organizing, but seeing it concretely shakes her.

"The Keepers wrote about this. There was a binding that was supposed to prevent the dead from maintaining consciousness after rising. The binding was placed in the Root-Ways themselves. But if the binding is failing…

"The dead would retain will. Intent. Purpose. They could be directed. They could work. They could think."

She looks at you with genuine fear: "If the dead can think, they can plan. And if they're being organized, then something with greater will than Níðhöggr's hunger is doing the organizing. Something intelligent. Something that wants the dead to act, not just consume."

This is a major realization. The dead are not a natural disaster. They are a tool. And Grafvitnir, trapped in the root-chamber, is using them.

The Direction Question

If the party can determine the column's direction safely, they realize it's moving east-southeast. Toward Rindgate. Toward the interior.

Why east? What's there?

The party doesn't know yet, but it's unsettling. The draugr aren't just migrating or spreading randomly. They're being sent somewhere.

Lew, if asked, might say: "East from here… that's toward the Interior Belt. Toward the mountains, and beyond. Toward Bone-Fields and beyond that. There's not much out there. A few settlements. Lot of dead places."

Kaelen might add: "Unless… unless it's not the settlements they're going for. Unless they're being sent to guard something. Or to protect something. Or to gather something."

The party is beginning to understand that their personal quest is not the only thing happening in Svellheim. Grafvitnir has his own plan, and it's moving on multiple fronts.

Part 5: The Deeper Passage

Continuing Into the Rot

As the party decides to move deeper into the caves (toward the exit that will eventually lead to Rindgate), the character of the caves changes.

The walls, which were clean stone before, now show signs of infection. Black veins spread across the carvings. The stone itself is weeping—a dark, oily substance that smells like decay and sulfur.

Kaelen recognizes this: The Rot.

It's not just a metaphor in the texts. It's real. Yggdrasil's roots are literally rotting, infected from the inside, spreading corruption outward.

The Root-Runes on the walls—the ancient carvings—are being obscured, overwritten by this spreading blight. Where it touches, the carefully carved symbols become corrupted, distorted.

If you're sensitive to the Wyrd, you feel it: the carvings are screaming. Silently, but the feeling is undeniable—anguish, despair, a fundamental wrongness spreading through the stone itself.

  1. Discovered the ancient cave system and its purpose (a library or archive of the Root-Ways).
  2. Encountered undead in organized formation, which shakes their understanding of what they're fighting.
  3. Seen The Rot spreading through the root-chamber, corrupting even ancient carvings.
  4. Realized that Grafvitnir is directing the dead, not just unleashing them.

They are deep in the caves, moving toward the passage that will lead them out near Rindgate. They are exhausted, wounded possibly, and growing afraid.

And ahead of them, in the deepest chamber (which they have not yet reached), someone very old is watching and waiting.

GM Notes

📝 Director Guidance

  • Pacing: This beat is best split into two sessions: one for the descent and grotto discovery, one for the wolf combat and draugr column observation.
  • Horror tone: Emphasize the wrongness of the organized draugr. It's more unsettling than a simple undead horde would be. Intelligence plus hunger is far scarier.
  • Kaelen's arc: She's beginning to understand that the Keepers were not the first, and that she carries knowledge of an ancient system she doesn't fully comprehend. This feeds into her eventual realization in Beat 15 that she is meant to be a living anchor to that system.
  • Foreshadowing: The Rot spreading through the Root-Runes. The dead moving east. Grafvitnir directing from the depths. All of this sets up the later encounters and the climax.
  • Resource reminder: Heat is being consumed. They're 4–5 days into caves. The Steam-Oasis is still many days north. Supplies are getting lower. The party should feel the pressure of scarcity.
  • Wyrd and magic: If your table uses magic or Wyrd-sense, make it resonate here. The ancient carvings, the bioluminescence, the anguished screams of the Root-Runes—these are all Wyrd infrastructure failing.
