Overview

Deep in the cave system, the party stumbles into the domain of Rickety Frets—Svellheim's oldest skald, a spirit who has been wandering the underwater caves for thousands of years, watching civilization rise and fall from beneath the world.

He is not hostile. He is not precisely friendly. He is old, wry, melancholic, and profoundly lonely. He has watched the dead organize for months, and it has disturbed him enough to break his perfect isolation.

The party discovers that Rickety Frets exists in a vast chamber filled with carved tablets, bone records, and the physical inscriptions of history itself. He is the archive. He is the witness to ages.

The negotiation here is not about convincing him to help through flattery or bribery. It's about reaching something in him that still cares whether the world continues. It's about offering him, perhaps for the first time in millennia, a reason to believe the story isn't over.

By the end of this beat, Rickety Frets becomes the party's guide through the rest of the cave system and provides critical intelligence about the draugr's destination.

Part 1: Discovery of the Library

Entering the Chamber

The party is moving through passages that are becoming less flooded, more dry. They are approaching the eastern side of the cave system—where the water returns to underground streams and the caves open into larger, air-filled chambers.

In one such chamber—vast, columned, with a ceiling lost in darkness—they find it.

Books. Carved tablets. Bone records. Thousands of them, covering every surface, stacked in careful piles, arranged in no obvious pattern except the pattern of time and obsessive care.

The walls are covered in handwriting—notes, marginalia, references, a scholar's chaos. The stone floor is covered in more tablets, bones inscribed with text, small carved wooden figures (historical records?), and in the center: a firepit, ancient, never quite dead, keeping the chamber at a survivable temperature.

It is, without question, a library. And it has been maintained with care for centuries.

The chamber opens before you like a cathedral made of words.

Stone columns rise from floor to ceiling—perhaps 30 feet high. Every surface between them and on the walls is covered in inscriptions. Some are carved deeply and precisely. Some are scratched hastily, as if someone was running out of time. Some are so old that weather and water have worn them nearly away.

On the floor: tablets arranged in spirals and grids. Bone fragments with text on both sides. Small, intricate carvings—miniature figures, scenes of battles, hunts, rituals. Thousands of small histories, all contributing to something vast and incomprehensible.

And on the eastern wall, a figure.

It stands motionless, facing away from you, one hand resting on the carved stone. At first, you think it's a statue. Then it turns.

It is humanoid. Tall, too tall perhaps, with a frame that seems to flicker between solid and translucent. Its face is weathered—not like an old man's, but like a stone that has stood in wind and rain for centuries. Its eyes are bright and aware.

When it speaks, its voice is dry, paper-thin, like pages turning in wind:

"I am Rickety Frets. I was Svellheim's historian when there was a Svellheim worth chronicling. Now I chronicle its ending. You are the first to find this place in... I have lost count of the years. Forgive me if I am not welcoming. I have not been a host in longer than anyone alive has lived."

The Spirit of Rickety Frets

NPC: Rickety Frets

Rickety Frets is a spirit—not a draugr (he is conscious and whole), not a ghost (he was never bound to a location against his will), but something older and stranger. A shade, perhaps, or what happens when someone lives so long and so consciously that the boundary between living and dead becomes unclear.

Appearance: Tall, thin, almost cadaverous. He wears robes that might once have been expensive, now faded to the color of old paper. His hands are stained with ink (or whatever spirits use for ink). His face is lined with the work of countless years. His eyes are the only thing that seem alive—sharp, observant, filled with a melancholy intelligence.

Manner: He is not rude, but he is not warm. He speaks in complete sentences, with precision. He has not had conversation in a very long time and is slightly rusty at the art of smalltalk. He tends to make observations that are too honest, comments that reveal more about his solitude than courtesy dictates.

His humor: Dry, self-aware, bitter. He makes jokes about the world ending, about how fitting it is that he would spend eternity in a library only to have the library's subject matter become increasingly focused on decline.

What He Wants to Know

Rickety Frets is intensely curious about the party, but not in a way they might expect. He doesn't care about their individual names or stories. He wants to know:

  1. What year is it? (Not the literal year—he knows that. But what age are they in? The Age of Embers? How many years have the Fimbulwinter lasted?)
  2. How is the world dying? (He has heard the draugr organizing; he wants confirmation of what he suspects.)
  3. Who are they, and why are they in his library? (This is more curiosity than concern.)
  4. Is there still a sky? (This is asked half-seriously. He is genuinely uncertain whether the surface world still exists or whether it has been completely consumed by ice.)

If the party answers his questions directly and honestly, he begins to warm—slightly. Not to friendship, but to something approximating professional interest.

Part 2: The Negotiation with Rickety Frets

Victory Points — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate (I3/P3) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

Victory Points — Type: Negotiation | Difficulty: Moderate (I3/P3) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

Structure: A Conversation, Not a Formal Negotiation

This is a Moderate Negotiation Encounter, but it should be played as a conversation, not a roll-by-roll exchange. The party and Rickety Frets are not enemies. They are scholars meeting across an impossible gulf of time.

Setup

Interest: 3 (Melancholy Curiosity) — He is not hostile, but he is profoundly unmotivated. Thousands of years of watching things end have eroded his sense of urgency. He will engage, but he will not help unless given a reason that matters to someone who has outlasted everything.

Patience: 3 — He has all the time in the world, but his patience is for interesting arguments. Small talk, flattery, and sentimentality bore him quickly.

📝 GM Notes: Draw Steel Negotiation Mechanics

Under Draw Steel rules, each argument is a power roll using Reason, Intuition, or Presence (with any applicable interpersonal skill). Motivations require a medium difficulty roll; arguments with no motivation or pitfall require a difficult roll.

Motivation appeal (medium difficulty):

  • Tier 1 (11 or lower): Patience −1. No Interest change.
  • Tier 2 (12–16): Interest +1, Patience −1.
  • Tier 3 (17+): Interest +1, Patience unchanged.

No motivation or pitfall (difficult):

  • Tier 1: Patience −1, Interest −1.
  • Tier 2: Patience −1 only.
  • Tier 3: Interest +1, Patience −1. (Natural 19–20: Patience unchanged.)

Pitfall invoked: Automatic failure. Interest −1, Patience −1.

With Patience 3 and Interest 3, the party needs to reach Interest 5 for Rickety Frets to agree to help ("Yes—I will guide you and share what I know"). Two successful Tier 2 motivation appeals will do it, or one Tier 3 appeal combined with a strong Tier 2. One pitfall invoked severely damages the negotiation. The party has room to maneuver, but wasted arguments quickly erode Patience.

Each motivation can only be successfully invoked once.

Motivations (medium difficulty roll)

  • The continuation of history: Appeal to Rickety Frets's role as a witness and keeper of records. Frame it as: "Historians record the present for the future. If there is a future, someone will need to know what happened. You've been waiting for someone to tell. We can be that witness." A historian without an audience is merely a custodian of a tomb. If the party can offer him that audience—the promise that his centuries of work will be read, witnessed, remembered—it speaks to something fundamental in him.
  • Understanding what's happening: Appeal to his genuine curiosity about the draugr and the Rot. He has seen them organizing, and this disturbs him—it violates the covenant he has witnessed. The dead are not supposed to be mindless and organized. Something is breaking the rules. The party can help him solve this mystery, can help him understand a fundamental wrongness in the world he has chronicled.
  • The possibility that the story isn't over: Appeal to hope, but do it carefully. Frame it not as naive optimism, but as a question worth asking: What if the world continues? What if there is a future? Rickety Frets has lived so long that he has despaired. But if the party can give him even a thin reason to believe that continuation is possible, that the story might have chapters left to write, it will move him.

Pitfalls (automatic failure — Interest −1, Patience −1)

  • Pity: Treating him as a sad old man worthy of sympathy. "You poor, lonely spirit. Let us help you." Rickety Frets does not want pity. He is ancient, learned, and still fully conscious. Reducing him to an object of compassion insults him and closes him off immediately.
  • Being treated as a tool: Using him without regard for his perspective or agency. "Help us, and then we're done with you" or "We need your knowledge; that's what matters." He sees through this instantly. He has been alone for millennia and knows the difference between conversation and transaction.
  • Dismissing his library or his work: Any suggestion that his centuries of preservation don't matter or that his records are irrelevant. "This is all just ancient history" or "The past doesn't matter now." This is the quickest way to anger him. His work is his existence. Dismissing it dismisses him.

Negotiation Flow

The PCs can discuss strategy between arguments (the "out of character planning" approach is explicitly supported by Draw Steel). Let them feel the weight of Patience 3—they have room to maneuver, but wasteful arguments add up.

Reason: Logical arguments work well here. Frame historical continuity as an intellectual concept, appeal to his scholarly sensibilities, cite examples from the library's own records.

Presence: Emotional appeals work if they avoid pity. Appeal to his isolation and the genuine relief of being heard and witnessed.

Intuition: Reading Rickety Frets's responses mid-negotiation. He is honest—if an argument isn't landing, he will say so. Intuition can be used to pivot when a line of argument isn't resonating.

Kaelen's potential contribution: If Kaelen has explored the Keeper records in the library and speaks about the continuity of the role, her voice carries weight. Rickety Frets respects someone who understands history deeply. If Kaelen makes an argument, she can add +1 to Reason or Presence tests.

Reading Rickety Frets's Responses

Use these to guide roleplay between arguments:

  • Interest goes up: He becomes quieter, more thoughtful. He may ask follow-up questions, showing that an idea has caught his attention. "You're right. I had not considered that angle." His ancient eyes brighten slightly—not with hope exactly, but with the recognition of something intellectually compelling.
  • Interest stays flat (no-motivation argument): He sighs, frustrated. "You're repeating yourself" or "That's not an argument; that's a wish." He withdraws slightly, returning his attention to the tablets around him—a sign that you're losing him.
  • Interest drops (pitfall): His voice becomes cold and distant. "I see. You don't understand what I am or what this place is." He turns away from the party, physically. The negotiation is on the edge of collapse.
  • Patience at 1: He stands. He begins to walk toward the deeper chambers of the library. The sense of time running out becomes acute. "I have indulged you long enough, I think. The records need tending."

The First Exchange

The party will likely ask: Can you help us? Can you guide us through the caves?

Rickety Frets's initial answer is: "I can. But why should I? I have guided no one in millennia. The world has moved on. Soon enough, the story ends—that is, it ends the way all stories end, in silence. Does it matter if you survive to see it?"

This is not a refusal. It's a test. He is genuinely asking them to convince him that survival, continuation, and the future matter.

Approach Examples

💡 Approach: Appeal to His Role as Historian

"You're a historian. Historians record the present for the future. If there is a future, someone will need to know what happened. You've been waiting for someone to tell. We can be that witness."

On success: He begins to see the party as potentially valuable to his life's work.

Example statement: "Hmm. A future. I had... I suppose I had given up on the notion. But you're right. A historian without anyone to read his work is merely a keeper of a tomb. Very well. Continue."

💡 Approach: Appeal to His Curiosity About the Draugr

"You've seen them organizing. We've seen it too. Something is directing them. We're trying to reach the source. Help us understand what's happening—and maybe we can stop it."

On success: Rickety Frets is disturbed by the draugr organizing, and he wants answers.

Example statement: "The dead were supposed to be mindless. That was the covenant. But they're not, are they? They march in columns. They carry tools. Something is teaching them, or teaching through them. Find me the answer, and I will help you. It will keep me sane during the last age."

💡 Approach: Appeal to His Solitude

"You've been alone for thousands of years. You're going to be alone whether we succeed or fail. Might as well have some company for the journey."

On success (Presence roll): He acknowledges that yes, he has been alone, and yes, that has worn on him. He becomes less dismissive.

On failure: He feels pitied, and he resents it. "I don't need your company, child. I need a reason to believe the world hasn't already ended."

💡 Approach: Ask Him About the Library

"What is all this? What did you chronicle? What is your work?"

Effect: This turns the conversation into something Rickety Frets genuinely wants to discuss. A montage of him explaining his library, his purpose. Less negotiation, more character building.

Result: If the party listens respectfully and asks thoughtful questions, Interest naturally increases. He sees them as potential appreciators of his life's work.

The Turning Point

Regardless of which approach works, the turning point comes when Rickety Frets says something like:

"The draugr I've been watching... they're not going south. They're not congregating at Hrafnborg. They're moving east and north. Toward the Interior Belt, toward the deep mountains, toward... I don't know where exactly. But they're being gathered for something. Someone is collecting the dead for a purpose."

He pauses, and his voice becomes quieter:

"I was afraid, at first. Afraid that whatever is organizing them was coming for me. But it's not. It's not interested in a solitary old spirit muttering to himself in the dark. It's interested in something else. Something bigger.

"I believe... I believe it's trying to prevent someone from reaching the north. Someone important. Someone who might undo what has been done."

At this point, you feel something shift. Rickety Frets is looking directly at you, and there's recognition in his ancient eyes.

Kaelen should feel seen. Rickety Frets is obliquely acknowledging that she is important, that her presence is significant, that her journey matters.

The Oath

📜 The Oath of Witness

Once the negotiation has progressed (assuming at least one successful argument or exchange), Rickety Frets makes an offer:

"I will guide you through the rest of the caves. I will tell you what I know of the draugr's movements and their destination. I will give you what provisions I have hoarded in this place. And in return, you will do two things:

1. You will remember me. You will tell someone—anyone—that Rickety Frets existed, that he watched over history, that his work was not in vain.

2. If the world is not ending, if there is a future, you will ensure that word reaches back here. Someone will need to know what became of the age."

He pauses. His ancient expression shifts — something harder, something more desperate, surfaces beneath the scholarly composure.

"And there is a third thing. The dead took something from me. A tablet — the @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn}, the Binding Record. They reached into my home with impossible arms and stole it from the shelf where it had rested for six hundred years."

"If you find it on the road ahead — in the hands of the dead, in a ruin, in a place where the organized columns have gathered — you will bring it back. Not to me, necessarily. I may not survive to receive it. But you will ensure it is not used against the binding. You will destroy it before letting Grafvitnir's servants use the knowledge it contains."

"This I ask because I cannot ask anyone else. And because you are going where the dead are going."

This is not a transactional trade. It is an oath — and not a simple one. The party is swearing to be witnesses, messengers, and now guardians of dangerous knowledge. The @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn} contains the mechanism of the binding itself. In the wrong hands, it reveals how to break the locks rather than renew them.

Oaths carry weight in Svellheim. If they make this oath and break it, they will feel the consequences (in Draw Steel terms, they lose Word permanently, and the Wyrd will hold them accountable).

📝 Director Note: The Third Demand's Weight

The first two demands (remember me, send word) are emotional but low-cost. The third demand (protect or destroy the @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn}) is material. It gives the party a tangible obligation — an artifact to track, recover, or destroy — that will follow them through Acts 2-3.

If the party later recovers the tablet during the side quest in this same beat, the oath is partially fulfilled. But Rickety Frets's phrasing ("if you find it on the road ahead") implies the obligation extends beyond this cave. If copies exist, if the knowledge has already been transmitted to Grafvitnir's agents, the party's duty to protect that knowledge persists.

This creates a real decision point at the Ivory Tower (Beat 15) and potentially at the Emberwell (Beat 19): what do they do with dangerous Keeper knowledge?

If the party accepts all three demands, Rickety Frets's Interest maxes at 5. He becomes, not a friend, but an ally. And more importantly, he becomes a witness. His presence — metaphorical now — will follow them through the rest of Act 2.

Part 3: The Hollow Record

The Gap in the Archive

As the party settles into Rickety Frets's library, exploring the vast expanse of carved tablets and bone records, an observant character (Intuition test TN 13) notices something odd: on one of the outer shelves, there is a precise rectangular impression in the dust. A tablet was resting here. Recently removed.

If the party asks about it, Rickety Frets becomes uncomfortable. He turns away. He is silent for a long moment before answering:

"Three weeks ago. A column of the organized dead paused outside this chamber. They did not enter—they respected the threshold, as if something had taught them to. But one of them, larger than the others, more... aware... it came to the entrance. It stood there, motionless. And then it reached inside, without crossing the boundary. Its arm was impossibly long. It reached directly to that shelf."

He does not look at the party when he speaks.

"It took one tablet. The @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn}. The Binding Record. I have been trying not to think about what that means. I have been cataloging the consequences. It does not help."

He pauses, then adds, very quietly: "I made a copy, long ago. For the archive. I transcribed it from an older source that has since crumbled to dust. It should still be here, if you wish to see what was taken."

The Bundingsteinn: The Binding Record

If the party asks to see the copy, or if they insist on understanding what was taken, Rickety Frets retrieves a tablet from a sealed chamber deeper in the library. It is bone, carved with precise, ancient script. The writing is in Old Svellheimer, but the party's scholar (if they have one) or Kaelen can translate.

The tablet describes a clause from the First Binding—the original covenant that bound Grafvitnir beneath the roots. It is the only surviving copy of the specific mechanism:

📜 The Bundingsteinn Translation

"When the root-heart fails, and the guardian-beast stirs, the binding may be renewed through the Ever-Flame. But the renewal is not simple rekindling. It is a transference. A living anchor must take root at the moment of the old binding's failure. The Keeper does not relight the flame. The Keeper becomes the flame. She takes the root into herself. She is bound as the beast is bound. When her light fails, so does the binding."

Additional marginal notes in a different hand (possibly Rickety Frets himself):

"The First Keepers understood this. The role was not a gift. It was a covenant with the roots themselves. Each Keeper was bound at the moment the previous Keeper's light failed. The world survived because the Keepers were willing to become something other than themselves. To be burned, slowly, for the world's sake."

⚠ Director Secret: Kaelen's Layer 2 Revealed

This is the moment when Kaelen fully understands the nature of her role. She reads the tablet. She reads it twice. Her face does not change, but something shifts in her eyes.

She does not share the realization with the party. Not yet. Not here.

But she now understands: When they reach the Emberwell, she will not light the flame and return home. The flame will light her. She will be bound as Grafvitnir is bound. She will become the anchor. And when her light fails, another Keeper must take her place, or the world ends.

The tablet is the intellectual precursor to the emotional revelation at the Ivory Tower. She has now seen the theory. She will feel its weight later.

The Draugr Column

Rickety Frets can trace the column through his knowledge of the cave network. The dead that took the tablet are still moving east—moving systematically, moving with purpose, carrying the @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn} deeper into the network. They are approximately 4–6 hours ahead of the party at current pace.

The column contains:

⚔️ The Draugr Column

  • 2 × @UUID[Actor.sCrszDJhuSeWPmsz]{Wyrd-Draugr} (Level 3 Elite, EV 20 each = 40) — commanders of the column; one carries the @UUID[Item.CBajVaiBXUskf4Wm]{Bundingsteinn}. They flee when below half Stamina.
  • 4 × @UUID[Actor.SrqOaBihqsBabzmG]{Wight-Commander} (Level 4 Platoon, EV 12 each = 48) — undead war-leaders that enforce formation discipline.
  • 4 × @UUID[Actor.kbrjiWLYy9tCJJjn]{Oath-Broken Draugr} (Level 3 Elite, EV 20 each = 80) — heavy infantry backbone of the column.
  • 9 × @UUID[Actor.1xAMhfQaEbKptK1a]{Frost-Draugr} (Level 2 Platoon, EV 8 each = 72) — rank and file, moving in tight formation.

Total EV: 240 (Hard band 231, partyES 220, Level 3 party)

Tactical Approaches: Three Paths Forward

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Optional - direct route only) | Difficulty: Moderate to Hard | VP Award: 1-2 VP on victory

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Optional - direct route only) | Difficulty: Moderate to Hard | VP Award: 1-2 VP on victory

Rickety Frets explains that the party can pursue the column—if they wish to reclaim the tablet. But he offers no single solution. Each approach carries risk.

💡 Approach A: Combat Ambush (Hard Difficulty)

Use Rickety Frets's knowledge to get ahead of the column via a flooded shortcut (a passage through partially submerged tunnels). Emerge ahead of the draugr and set an ambush in a narrow section of the cave system.

Combat Difficulty: Hard (EV 240 — 2x Wyrd-Draugr + 4x Wight-Commander + 4x Oath-Broken Draugr + 9x Frost-Draugr).

Tactical Complication: The @UUID[Actor.sCrszDJhuSeWPmsz]{Wyrd-Draugr} is intelligent. It recognizes the party as a threat. Its primary concern is not fighting to the death—it is preserving the tablet. If the Wyrd-Draugr takes significant damage (10+ stamina lost in a single round, or half its total stamina), it will attempt to flee deeper into the network. Once it escapes, the party loses the tablet permanently (the Wyrd-Draugr will deposit it at the next waypoint in the network, far beyond reach).

Reward: Immediate possession of the tablet. Control of the artifact. The draugr column is disrupted.

💡 Approach B: Stealth Retrieval (Skill Challenge)

Shadow the column at a distance. Identify which creature carries the tablet (Intuition test TN 14 to spot the leather case) without triggering an alarm. Execute a theft without combat.

Skill Challenge: This requires 2 successful Agility or Intuition tests (Moderate difficulty TN 13), or 1 Agility Tier 3 success, or 1 Intuition Tier 3 success. A character can make the test once; assistance from another character grants edge or bane per Draw Steel rules.

Failure: The attempted theft alerts the column. Combat begins immediately—but the @UUID[Actor.sCrszDJhuSeWPmsz]{Wyrd-Draugr} will attempt to flee with the tablet, creating a chase sequence rather than a stand-and-fight engagement.

Reward: The party obtains the tablet without expending resources on combat. The draugr column continues east, unaware they've been stolen from, and the party can rest before moving on.

💡 Approach C: Decoy and Distraction (Creative Use of Resources)

Kaelen's ember-mark radiates warmth that the dead are instinctively drawn to. She can deliberately amplify this signal, creating a false beacon in a side passage, causing the @UUID[Actor.sCrszDJhuSeWPmsz]{Wyrd-Draugr} and the majority of its column to investigate while the party extracts the tablet from the remaining guards.

Requirements:

  • Kaelen must spend 1 Recovery (she exhausts herself amplifying the mark).
  • Kaelen makes a Presence test TN 13 to successfully "sell" the distraction (the dead must believe the signal is genuine, not a trick). On success, both Wyrd-Draugr and 4 Wight-Commanders divert to investigate.

Combat Consequence: The remaining 4 Oath-Broken Draugr + 9 Frost-Draugr are encountered. This is a significantly easier combat (EV ~150) than engaging the full column.

Advantage: The Wyrd-Draugr is split between protecting the tablet and investigating the warmth signal. If the party defeats it (or pins it down), they obtain the tablet before it can flee.

Disadvantage: Kaelen is exhausted after the amplification. The party loses a Recovery and Kaelen's full combat effectiveness for the encounter.

Rickety Frets's Response Upon Return

If the party retrieves the tablet (by any method), they return to Rickety Frets. He receives it with a complex expression—something between relief and renewed sorrow.

He examines it carefully, confirms its identity, and then sits down at a small stone desk. He begins to carve a new copy into a fresh tablet, working with practiced precision. This takes approximately one hour.

When he finishes, he hands the original tablet to Kaelen directly. He looks at her when he does so, holds her gaze for a long moment. He does not say: "I think you already know what this means." But the unspoken words hang in the air between them.

If Kaelen asks him to explain what it means, Rickety Frets will be reluctant, but he will answer:

"The First Keepers understood the covenant. They knew the role would consume them. The flame does not burn without fuel. The anchor does not hold without sacrifice. Each Keeper was bound knowing that their predecessor had been burned away. Knowing that they, too, would be burned. Knowing that another would come after them to be burned in turn."

He pauses, returning to his work, carving slowly.

"If you are asking whether I think you have understood what the Emberwell requires... yes. I think you understand. I think you have understood for some time. I think you are wondering whether to tell the others, and I think you have decided not to. Not yet. Not until you must."

He does not look up. "That is the burden of being the last. The knowledge comes alone. The choice comes alone. Everything else comes later."

If the Party Fails to Retrieve the Tablet

If the party decides not to pursue the column, or if their attempts all fail, the tablet is lost. Rickety Frets does not express anger, but there is a profound sadness in him.

He will still provide the copy he made, and he will still guide the party north. But something has shifted. He understands, more clearly now, that the forces moving against the party are organized, coordinated, and have knowledge of what matters.

He will warn them: "Whatever is organizing the dead knows what you carry. It knows what Kaelen is. It is moving to prevent you from reaching the north. Be careful. Be very careful. The Rot does not move randomly. Someone is directing it. And that someone wants to ensure that the Emberwell stays dark."

Part 5: The Library and the Archive

Exploring the Collection

If the party has time (and if they've made peace with Rickety Frets), they can explore his library. This serves several purposes:

  1. Provides lore about Svellheim.
  2. Gives Kaelen a chance to discover things she didn't know about her own tradition.
  3. Builds atmosphere and a sense of the weight of history.

The library is organized, but only Rickety Frets fully understands the system. However, the party can identify broad sections:

The histories: Tablets describing major events—the Age of Roots, the Silver Era, the Wolf Era, the Long Night. Carved in precise language, these are the "official" records.

The personal accounts: Tablets and bones with more hastily carved text—individual stories, witness accounts, legends that didn't make the official histories. These are more human, more emotional. Some contradict the official record.

The chronicles of the Keepers: A specific section, clearly maintained with even more care than the rest. Records of the Seven Ember-Keepers, their rites, their studies, their failures.

The prophecies and warnings: Tablets that speak of a time when the roots would sicken, when the guardian-beasts would stir, when the world would need to be renewed. Some are vague. Some are specific enough to be unsettling.

Kaelen's Discovery

If Kaelen explores the Keeper section, she finds something that shakes her:

A record—carved in the hand of one of the First Keepers (perhaps 1500 years ago)—that describes the ritual of the Ever-Ember, and in particular, a note about the Keeper who first performed the rite:

"Sigrid took the seed of the first flame and brought it into her body. The warmth was infinite, burning, divine. She did not survive the joining as we understand survival. She transformed. She became the conduit. Her life became the flame's life. Her death became the flame's death—eternal and slow, burning the whole of the world instead of burning in a single hearth. She was not remembered as she had been. She was remembered as the Eternal Flame itself."

You watch Kaelen read these words. She goes very quiet. She reads it twice. She traces the carved words with her finger as if she can read more through touch.

Then she says, barely audibly: "The Keepers didn't keep the flame. They became the flame. And when they could no longer serve, they passed the role on. Not like a crown. Like a wound that could only be healed by someone new offering themselves."

She doesn't tell you the full implication. Not yet. But something has shifted in her understanding.

⚠ Director Secret: Kaelen's Realization

Kaelen now understands: the Emberwell is not a destination where they will light a flame and return home. It is a place where the role is transferred. And if she is the last Keeper, and if Yggdrasil is expecting her...

The story is ending. And she is the ending.

She will not share this realization with the party until Beat 19.

The Warning

⚠ Director Secret: The Coordinated Defense

"The draugr column I mentioned—the one moving east. It's not the only one. There are at least three major movements I've sensed through the Root-Ways. They're converging. Gathering at a place... somewhere in the eastern interior. A place with old magic. Old defenses. The undead are being positioned to guard something, or to prevent something from reaching somewhere."

He pauses, then adds: "The woman who came with you—Kaelen. Is she the Keeper?"

Honest answer: Yes.

"Then the dead are moving to prevent her from reaching wherever she's going. Someone knows. Someone has planned this. And the plan is already in motion."

This is a heavy moment. The party is not simply being hunted. They are being cordoned off. Grafvitnir is trying to stop them from reaching the Emberwell.

Part 6: Supplies and Provisions

What Rickety Frets Offers

The ancient historian has hoarded supplies over the centuries. Much of it is useless—dried food from ancient stores, weapons that have rusted beyond use, clothing that crumbles at a touch. But some remains:

  • Preserved fish and salted meat: Enough to provision the party for several more days.
  • Sealed alchemical vials: Unknown contents, but clearly preserved with care. A character with Alchemy or Arcana can attempt to identify them (Moderate difficulty). Most contain medicines or stimulants—useful for recovering stamina or resisting cold.
  • Maps: Old, inaccurate, but showing the cave system's general layout. These are worth something. They show that the caves extend far north and east, beyond the immediate chamber.
  • A light source: @UUID[Item.5mM6huJQDr6keS0L]{Bioluminescent Lantern} — A magical lantern, ancient and faintly glowing with a blue-green bioluminescence. Unlike torches, it won't burn out, but it's dimmer and somehow unsettling to look at directly.
  • A journal: Rickety Frets's own chronicle, from which he will not part, but from which he will read passages. These passages should be poignant, personal, and reinforce that he has watched the entire arc of civilization with meticulous attention.

Heat cost: These provisions cost the party nothing to take, but they gain a small amount of Heat (supplies improve rest efficiency by 1–2 points).

Part 7: The Departure

A Moment of Melancholy

As the party prepares to leave, Rickety Frets becomes quiet. He looks around his library, at the thousands of tablets and records and the lifetime of work.

"If the world does end," he says, "at least someone will have tried to stop it. That is what the best stories are—people trying, against all odds, to prevent the ending."

He walks with you to the exit of his chamber, and gives you one final piece of advice:

"The road ahead goes through the Bone-Fields. The dead rise there without guidance—there's something wrong with that place, something that predates Grafvitnir's work. Be careful. The boundary between the worlds is thin there, thinner than even here in the deep."

Then he returns to his library, to his work, to his solitude. You will not see him again.

But his presence—his oath-witness, his belief in continuity—becomes part of your journey. You carry the weight of his loneliness with you, and the weight of his hope.

The Naming

Before you go, Rickety Frets asks: "What are your names? I have not had anyone to name since the Silver Era. It would be... it would be a courtesy if I knew who it is I'm sending into the dark."

The party should give their names. Rickety Frets repeats each one carefully, as if carving it onto a tablet in his memory:

"[Name]. [Name]. [Name]. [Name]. I will add you to my chronicle. The party that sought the Emberwell. When the world remembers, it will remember you."

This is small, but it is significant. It is an acknowledgment that your names matter, that your quest matters, that you are not simply playing out a predetermined ending.

GM Notes

📝 Director Guidance

  • Rickety Frets's voice: He should sound tired, wise, and slightly frustrated with the living world's business. His dialog should reflect someone who has had to become articulate through centuries of solitude, and who is slightly rusty at conversation. He might ramble, might go on tangents about history, might interrupt himself with asides.
  • The negotiation difficulty: This should not be a hard negotiation. It's a test of whether the party can meet Rickety Frets on his terms—as people who care about continuity and history, not as people asking a convenient NPC for a favor. If the party approaches it with respect and genuine interest, Interest rises naturally.
  • Kaelen's arc: This is where Layer 2 is fully revealed to her. She discovers the nature of the Keeper role and understands that she is meant to fulfill it. She does not share this with the party. Not yet. Not until Beat 19.
  • Atmosphere: The library should feel vast, heavy with history, and deeply sad. It's a place where one person has preserved the entire memory of a civilization, alone, waiting for someone to read it. The tragedy of it should be palpable.
  • Resource management: This is the last major supply opportunity before the Bone-Fields and the final stretch north. The party should feel that they are well-provisioned but running on limited reserves.
  • The oath: This is important for later. The party has sworn to remember Rickety Frets and to ensure his work is witnessed. This is a Wyrd obligation that carries weight through the rest of the campaign.

Key NPCs: Jarl Vigmund Haldorsson, refugees, scouts
Type: Settlement scene, social encounter, environmental reconnaissance, Grafvitnir Pressure check
Tone: Isolation and desperation; the frontier mentality; the sense that they have crossed into a new region with new dangers

Overview

The party emerges from the cave system near Rindgate (row 19, col 35)—a frontier settlement at the eastern foot of Midgard's Shield. They are exhausted from the caves, soaked, covered in bioluminescence residue, and bewildered by daylight.

Rindgate is not a warm welcome. The town is overcrowded with refugees from the interior, the Pale Maw has been raiding from the north, and trust is a luxury nobody can afford. The party arrives as mysterious strangers from the west, claiming to have come through an underground passage.

This beat is brief and atmospheric. It serves primarily to:

  1. Orient the party geographically to a new region (the eastern interior).
  2. Establish the isolation and desperation of frontier settlements.
  3. Provide reconnaissance opportunities for what lies ahead (particularly Hornvatn and Urdvatn, which are wrong in ways the party should be suspicious of).
  4. Generate Grafvitnir Pressure as the network becomes aware of their location.

By the end of Beat 13, the party has gathered supplies, gathered intelligence about the interior, and formed a clearer picture of what lies ahead: the Bone-Fields (east-southeast), the route north, and the gathering storm.

Part 1: The Emergence

Exit from the Caves

The underwater passage eventually leads to a series of air-filled tunnels that ascend. The party climbs, gradually leaving the waterlogged darkness behind.

The final chamber opens onto a rocky promontory overlooking an inland valley. The caves exit here, high enough that the party has a view of the surrounding landscape.

It is afternoon, and the sun is low and pale. The sky is white with cloud and the promise of more snow. The wind is sharp, carrying the smell of stone and ice.

You emerge from darkness into a world of white.

The day is fading. The sun, pale and distant, hangs low in the southern sky. The land around you is a stark white punctuated by exposed gray stone. A few scrubby plants—ancient, dwarfed by cold—cling to crevices in the rock.

Below you, a town sprawls on a hillside. Rindgate. The name comes to you from Lew's teachings months ago. The eastern frontier. The pass-town where the interior roads converge before they brave the mountains or turn north.

The walls are good stone and well-kept. The watchtowers are manned. But around the town, outside the walls, you see camps. Tents. Crude shelters. People huddled around small fires, their possessions piled beside them. Refugees. Hundreds of them, or more.

The mountain rises beyond the town—Midgard's Shield, the great barrier. To the east, the land descends into a valley, then rises again into distant foothills.

And there, in the middle distance: two lakes. One is frozen solid, smooth as glass. The other... the other has strange patches where the ice does not quite hold, where steam rises, where something beneath the surface seems to move.

Hornvatn and Urdvatn. And something is wrong with them.

Kaelen, beside you, makes a small sound—not quite a gasp, but close. She's looking at the frozen lake. Her hand is gripping your shoulder.

"Feel that?" she whispers. "That pull? Like something down there is... aware. Watching."

The Descent into Town

The party makes their way down from the caves toward Rindgate. This descent is not difficult (it's mostly a walk down the hillside), but it draws attention.

A watch-post on the eastern wall of Rindgate spots them approaching—six strangers, emerging from the wilderness, covered in cave-slime and looking half-drowned.

By the time the party reaches the town gates, they are met by armed men and women. Not hostile, not yet, but alert. Hands are on weapons. Eyes are measuring.

The initial challenge: "State your business. What brings you to Rindgate from the east? The mountains are closed to commerce. The caves to the west are not a trade route."

This is where the party's story matters. They can be honest (came through the caves from the coast), can lie (traders from the interior), or can be vague (refugees seeking shelter). Depending on their answer, they will face different levels of suspicion.

⚠ Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Roll 2)

Make a secret d6 roll:

  • 1 = nothing yet. The town is suspicious but not hostile. They'll let the party in for the night.
  • 2–4 = network interference. A Pale Maw informant is in town. Word is spreading that the party is here. Not immediate danger, but tension is rising.
  • 5–6 = major intervention. A @UUID[Actor.oB43PXxhrMlGnq7G]{Pale Maw Operative} is waiting in Rindgate, expecting the party to arrive from this direction. They will attempt to intercept or identify the party before the party can resupply and leave.

Part 2: Rindgate Settlement

The Town Layout and Atmosphere

Rindgate is a frontier town in slow collapse:

  • The walls are well-built stone, but undermanned. There are maybe 20-30 guards total, stretched thin.
  • The interior is wooden buildings, packed tightly. There's no zoning—homes, trading posts, forges, all mixed together. The streets are narrow, made narrower by the press of people.
  • Outside the walls, hundreds of people have gathered seeking shelter or passage south. They are from interior settlements—Beinvaka, settlements in the Bone-Fields, people fleeing from the draugr organizing. They have nowhere else to go.
  • A central plaza serves as the market where trade happens. Food is expensive and sparse. A loaf of bread costs 2-3 times what it did in Hrafnborg. Fuel costs even more.

NPC: Jarl Vigmund Haldorsson

Pragmatic frontier leader, 50s-60s.

The town is commanded by Jarl Vigmund Haldorsson. Practical armor bearing the wear of real use, iron-grey beard, world-weary eyes. He has held Rindgate together not by being heroic but by being practical, day after day.

Current Crisis: Refugees are overwhelming the town's resources. There isn't enough food. The Pale Maw has been raiding from the north. The mountain passes to the east are becoming unreliable.

Role: Vigmund is stretched thin, holding a town against impossible odds. Strangers are a complication, not a welcome.

Entering the Town

When the party enters, the town does not roll out a red carpet. They are locked in with a garrison member and escorted to Vigmund's headquarters—a stone building that serves as both command center and mead hall.

Vigmund's first questions are practical:

  1. Where are you from?
  2. Where are you going?
  3. Do you carry food? Do you carry weapons? Do you carry coin?
  4. Are you diseased? Are you cursed? Are you draugr-touched?

The last question is asked seriously. People in the far north have learned that sometimes the dead move like the living, speak like the living. They take precautions.

📝 GM Notes: Reading Vigmund

A Presence test (moderate) reveals Vigmund's genuine concern: this is someone trying to hold a town together against impossible odds, and strangers are a complication. Do not play him as hostile—only as defensive and exhausted.

Part 3: Intelligence and Preparation

What the Party Can Learn

If the party spends time in Rindgate (an hour or more, a night if they rest), they can gather intelligence:

Local Intelligence

From the refugees:

  • The draugr are moving. Organized columns marching northeast.
  • Settlements in the Bone-Fields have been abandoned or overrun.
  • Something is directing the dead. It's not natural.
  • A few refugees mention seeing "markers" placed in the Bone-Fields—piles of stone, carved symbols—that seem to guide the draugr.

From the garrison:

  • The Pale Maw has been raiding from the north, from Beinvaka direction.
  • The lakes (Hornvatn and Urdvatn) are both wrong. Hornvatn is solid ice but something moves beneath. Urdvatn's ice is unreliable—thin patches where steam rises.
  • The town tries not to go near either lake. There's no gain. It's just waste.
  • The route north from Rindgate goes through the Bone-Fields, then toward the interior highlands. It's cold, haunted, and increasingly dangerous.

From Vigmund (if negotiation is successful):

  • The Pale Maw operatives who have been raiding are different from the ones reported from the south. These are true believers, not mercenaries.
  • Word is spreading that a Keeper and a group of companions are heading north. The Pale Maw wants to intercept them.
  • Vigmund has heard rumors (from scouts, refugees) that the dead are being gathered for a purpose. They're not just spreading. They're being collected.

🐦 Dreyfus Thread: The Raven on the Wall

Traders arriving from the south mention a large, strange raven that has been circling Rindgate's walls for the past few days. It never lands, never feeds, and never calls — it simply watches. The garrison has tried to shoot it down twice; both arrows passed clean through. If the party asks further, one refugee swears they saw the bird perch on a rooftop and its shadow looked wrong — too large, and shaped like a dog.

Director note: This is pure thread-maintenance. Dreyfus (in raven form) is tracking the party's progress but will not intervene until Beat 24. No mechanical consequences — just a reminder that something is watching.

Grafvitnir Pressure Resolution

⚠ Pressure Outcomes

If the roll was 1 (nothing): No immediate danger. The party can rest, resupply, and prepare.

If the roll was 2–4 (interference): A Pale Maw informant is in town. They may approach the party directly (a disguised operative asking questions), or they may report back to their superiors that the party is here. The party can discover this with a good Perception test (spotting someone watching them, overhearing a conversation). If discovered, the operative will deny everything, but word will spread that the party is here. Vigmund may suggest they leave quickly.

If the roll was 5–6 (major intervention): A Pale Maw strike team (3-4 @UUID[Actor.oB43PXxhrMlGnq7G]{Pale Maw Operative}s + a leader) is in town waiting for the party. They will attempt to identify the party and either capture or kill them. This becomes a combat encounter or a tense social scene, depending on how the party handles it.

Combat Option: The Pale Maw try to grab the party quietly in their lodgings or on the street. The party must fight their way out and leave town.

Social Option: The party notices the operatives before being directly confronted. They can negotiate, disguise themselves, or slip out of town using back routes.

Part 4: The Lakes and Local Concerns

Hornvatn and Urdvatn

Kaelen felt something when she saw the lakes. The party should investigate this, or at least ask about them.

Hornvatn (the frozen lake): This lake is solid ice. It has been solid for months, unbroken. Except for one detail: if the party looks closely, or if they scout near it, they will see that there are depressions in the ice—regular, spaced intervals, as if something has been walking across the lake, over and over, in the exact same path. There are no footprints (the ice is too hard, the wind has scoured them), just the wear marks of repeated passage. Something is crossing Hornvatn. Regularly. With purpose.

Urdvatn (the uncertain lake): This lake has patches of thin ice, places where steam rises. It's not safe to cross. But if scouts or the party investigate, they will see movement beneath the water—not the random motion of fish, but the deliberate movements of something large. Something that responds to stimulus (if the party breaks through the ice, they can hear it moving, responding to the sound).

⚠ The Lakes Are Wrong

These lakes are wrong. They are both placeholders for something, signposts that mark the eastern interior as different from the western and southern lands.

Kaelen's Recognition

When Kaelen learns about the lakes (from refugees, from Vigmund, from her own sensing), she becomes troubled:

"These lakes aren't normal. I can feel the Rot in them. The boundary between the worlds... it's thin here. Very thin. Whatever is in the water, whatever is walking on the ice—it's not entirely in our world. It's not entirely out of it."

She looks at the party seriously:

"We don't go near the lakes. We go around them, carefully, and we don't look back. If what's in them realizes we've seen it, it will follow. I'm sure of that."

Kaelen's Growing Connection

This is a moment of character revelation. Kaelen is learning to trust her connection to the Wyrd, to the world-tree's systems. She is warning the party that there are powers at work that are older and stranger than the merely undead.

Skirting the Lakes (Short Montage)

Kaelen insists: they go around the lakes, not between them. But "around" is not safe either. The terrain between Rindgate and the northeastern road forces the party close to both shorelines. The ice groans. The wind carries sounds that aren't wind.

📝 GM Notes: Running This Encounter

This encounter is triggered by the montage result (unless Total Success was achieved). The Lakewalkers are ancient draugr frozen into a patrol loop — they have walked the same path across Hornvatn for centuries, wearing grooves into the ice. They are not servants of Grafvitnir. They predate his work. They are something older, something tied to the thin boundary between Helheim and Midgard in this region.

They do not speak. They do not react to threats, pain, or attempts at communication. They simply walk — and destroy anything between them and their path. This should be unsettling. They are not angry. They are not hunting. They are executing a route, and the party is in the way.

Lakewalker Draugr — Tactical Summary

Use the @UUID[Actor.a0TWAw8EFdEJ8YND]{Lakewalker Draugr} stat block for full details. Key tactical notes:

  • Relentless Patrol: If not engaged in melee at the start of its turn, it moves toward its patrol path (the nearest point on Hornvatn's ice). It attacks anything in its way but does not pursue creatures that step aside.
  • Ice-Bound: Resistant to cold damage. Vulnerable to fire damage (takes double damage from fire).
  • Unresponsive: Immune to fear, charm, and any effect that requires communication or comprehension. Cannot be negotiated with, intimidated, or reasoned with.
  • Frozen Shell: When reduced to 0 Stamina, the Lakewalker freezes in place and becomes a statue of ice. It does not collapse. It simply stops, mid-stride, and remains standing. (This is cosmetic but deeply unsettling.)

Tactical Notes: These are not clever combatants. They march. They attack what's in front of them. They don't flank, don't retreat, don't coordinate. Their danger is their inevitability — they will keep coming until destroyed. Use their straight-line movement to force the party to reposition.

Special Rules:

  • If the montage resulted in Failure, each Lakewalker begins 2 squares closer to the party than normal starting positions.
  • If a Lakewalker reaches Hornvatn's surface during combat, it resumes its patrol loop and ignores the party unless they re-enter its path.

Objective: Destroy or evade the Lakewalkers. The party does not need to kill them — retreating out of their patrol zone also works.

Aftermath: Urdvatn Stirs

Whether the party fought the Lakewalkers or passed unnoticed, this moment occurs as they move past Urdvatn:

You are past the worst of it. Hornvatn is behind you — the frozen figures either destroyed or still marching their endless route. The wind is quieter here.

Then Kaelen stops.

She is staring at Urdvatn. The uncertain lake. Its surface is a patchwork of thin ice and dark water, steam rising from cracks where the heat below meets the cold above.

Something moves beneath the surface. Not fish. Not current. Something large, deliberate, and aware. It shifts under the ice like a whale rolling in its sleep — a shadow the size of a longship, darker than the water around it.

It passes directly beneath a patch of thin ice. The ice bows upward. Does not break. Bows, and settles.

Then it is gone.

Kaelen says nothing. She turns away from the lake and walks faster.

📝 GM Notes: The Urdvatn Entity

Do not explain this. Do not give it a name. Do not have Kaelen explain what she sensed. This is foreshadowing — something ancient lives in Urdvatn, something that is aware of the party but chose not to act. Whether this entity becomes relevant later in the campaign is up to you. For now, it is a mystery and a source of dread.

If the party investigates (breaks ice, casts abilities, tries to communicate), they get nothing conclusive — ripples, cold, the sense of being watched, and then silence. The entity does not engage. It simply knows they are there.

Part 5: Supplies and Preparation

A Familiar Caravan

When the party enters Rindgate's market area, they find a surprise: Söldís Glóðfari's caravan, camped at the edge of the town square. The wagons are arranged in their usual careful circle, but this time they've taken on a semi-permanent quality — canvas awnings extended, trade goods displayed, a small repair station set up beneath the central wagon.

The caravan cannot follow the party north. The children — Einar and Svanhildr — cannot survive the road ahead. But Söldís has positioned herself at the last safe point, helping Rindgate's people with trade and supplies.

You see her before she sees you — or at least, that's what you think. Söldís is standing at a makeshift trade counter, negotiating with a Rindgate merchant over a bolt of heavy wool. She looks exactly the same as the last time you saw her: copper-brown braid, patchwork coat, the sealed clay pot glowing faintly at her throat.

Then she turns, and you realize she knew you were there the whole time.

"I was wondering when you'd catch up," she says. The smile is genuine. "You look worse than last time. That's progress — it means you haven't stopped moving."

The Caravan's Rindgate Role:

Söldís has been trading fairly with Vigmund's people — bringing in goods from the south in exchange for what can be spared. Her presence has stabilized Rindgate's supply situation slightly. Vigmund trusts her, which is rare for him.

Individual Caravan Moments

Ketil (the blacksmith) has set up a repair forge near the caravan. He's working steadily on tools and weapons for the town. If the party has damaged equipment, he can repair or improve one weapon or piece of armour: "I don't take coin. Bring me something to mend. That's all I know how to do anymore." His hands are sure, his voice isn't. His wife Ástríðr watches from the wagon, one hand on Svanhildr's shoulder.

Hallur (the former Chainwarden) approaches the party quietly, away from the others. He has intelligence about the road north — Chainwarden patrol routes, checkpoint schedules, which passes are watched and which are abandoned. "I know the routes because I used to run them," he says. "Don't ask me more than that. But if you're going through the Bone-Fields, go at first light. The patrols change shifts at dawn and there's a forty-minute gap." This grants +1 edge on one navigation or stealth test in Beat 14.

Dagur (the merchant) has connections with the broader trade network. If the party wants to know about supply conditions further north — what settlements are still standing, what roads are passable, what the Pale Maw's supply lines look like — he provides information grudgingly, as if doing the party a personal favour. "I don't do this for free, normally," he says. "But Söldís asked, so here we are."

Óttar is restless and visibly jealous of the party's journey. He has tried twice to follow the party's rumoured route north without permission. The second time, Söldís stopped him — and the caravan saw her genuinely angry for the first time: "You do not follow people on roads you cannot read." If the party talks to him, he asks pointed questions about the north. He's not stupid. He's just young enough to think courage and curiosity are the same thing.

Svanhildr (6, the "lucky" child) has not been sick once since joining the caravan. The other caravan members have started treating her like a blessing. When she sits by the party's fire, the warmth seems a little more reliable, the shadows a little more distant. This is probably coincidence. Probably.

What Söldís Asks

Before the party leaves, she repeats the question from their first meeting: "If you find a flame that shouldn't still be burning — don't put it out."

This time, if the party has context to ask better questions, she offers a fragment more: "I've been carrying a fire for longer than I can explain. I know what it means when a flame shouldn't exist but does. Don't make the mistake of thinking because something is impossible, it deserves to die."

She will not say more than this. She touches her sealed pot. The glow inside it seems to pulse, once, in time with something far away.

Mechanical Benefits

  • Ketil repairs or improves one piece of equipment (free)
  • Hallur's intelligence: +1 edge on one navigation or stealth test in Beat 14
  • Dagur's trade network: +1 edge on one knowledge test about northern conditions
  • Söldís provides supplies at fair prices (bypassing Rindgate's inflated rates for up to 3 days of rations)

What Rindgate Offers

The town has supplies, but they are expensive and limited:

  • Food is available, but prices are high. A week's rations (for 4 people) costs roughly 3x normal price.
  • Fuel is even more expensive. A day's worth of fuel (for heat and cooking) for 4 people costs 2-3 coin.
  • Rope, tools, and basic gear are available at normal prices.
  • Weapons and armor are available, but limited selection and high prices.
  • Healing supplies are scarce. A character can purchase one potion of healing or equivalent (costs 1-2 coin). More than that, and Vigmund will refuse—the town needs to keep its own supplies.

Heat and Word Costs

Resting in Rindgate costs normal Heat (the town has fire, shelter, and warmth). But Word is strained. Vigmund will help the party, but every favor costs Word—asking for discounted supplies, asking for information, asking for protection. The party can spend Word to reduce Heat costs or to gain better information.

Vigmund's Negotiation (Optional)

Victory Points — Type: Negotiation (Optional) | Difficulty: Moderate (I4/P4) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

Victory Points — Type: Negotiation (Optional) | Difficulty: Moderate (I4/P4) | VP Award: 1 VP on success

If the party is friendly and Vigmund takes a liking to them, he might propose an offer. This is a Moderate Negotiation Encounter.

Setup

Interest: 4 (Hopeful) — Vigmund is a pragmatic leader who respects the party's presence and capability. He is not hostile, but he is testing whether they have the fortitude to succeed.
Patience: 4 — Vigmund has time to discuss, but he has a town to run. Too much dithering, and he will move on to other matters.

📝 GM Notes: Draw Steel Negotiation Mechanics

Under Draw Steel rules, each argument is a power roll using Reason, Intuition, or Presence (with any applicable interpersonal skill). Motivations require a medium difficulty roll; arguments with no motivation or pitfall require a difficult roll.

Motivation appeal (medium difficulty):

  • Tier 1 (11 or lower): Patience −1. No Interest change.
  • Tier 2 (12–16): Interest +1, Patience −1.
  • Tier 3 (17+): Interest +1, Patience unchanged.

No motivation or pitfall (difficult):

  • Tier 1: Patience −1, Interest −1.
  • Tier 2: Patience −1 only.
  • Tier 3: Interest +1, Patience −1. (Natural 19–20: Patience unchanged.)

Pitfall invoked: Automatic failure. Interest −1, Patience −1.

With Patience 4 and Interest 4, Vigmund is already favorably inclined. The negotiation is less about convincing him and more about cementing an oath. The party only needs to reach Interest 5 to secure his full support (maps, supplies, and the oath). A single successful Tier 2 motivation appeal does it. Pitfalls are rare here—Vigmund is not hostile. The main risk is the party saying something that makes him doubt their sincerity.

Each motivation can only be successfully invoked once.

Motivations (medium difficulty roll)

  • Shared responsibility for the future: Vigmund has spent his whole life holding Rindgate together, protecting his people. Frame the party's quest as a continuation of that same work: "You're trying to save your town. We're trying to save the world. It's the same commitment, just at different scales. If we succeed, your people survive. If we fail, you lose everything anyway." Vigmund respects people who understand the weight of duty. This appeals to his sense of kinship with the party.
  • The power of witness and word: Appeal to his desire to be remembered, to matter. "You're holding a town together against the end of the world. That's a story worth telling. We will tell it. We will carry word of Jarl Vigmund—how he held his ground, how he made hard choices, how he believed there was a future." Vigmund is isolated and exhausted. The promise that his work will be witnessed and remembered is powerful.
  • Practical self-interest: The simplest appeal. "If we reach the Emberwell and the Fimbulwinter breaks, Rindgate becomes a refuge, a center of resettlement. You become a leader of the renewal, not just a keeper of a dying town. Your authority grows. Your people survive better." Vigmund is pragmatic. This argument speaks directly to his long-term interests.

Pitfalls (automatic failure)

  • False promises or bravado: "We will definitely win. The Fimbulwinter is as good as broken." Vigmund has lived through enough wars and crises to know when someone is lying to him. False confidence insults his intelligence. He closes off immediately.
  • Dismissing the plight of Rindgate: "Your refugees are not our concern" or "Rindgate is too far south to matter." Vigmund will see this as callous and selfish. He respects people who care about their communities, not people who use others as stepping stones.
  • Refusing or hesitating on the oath: If the party seems reluctant to swear to remember Rindgate and send word back, Vigmund will interpret this as disrespect. "You want my supplies and my support, but you won't commit to honoring what we've done here?" This breaks the negotiation.

Negotiation Flow

This negotiation should feel less like a combat of words and more like a conversation between people who respect each other. Vigmund is testing whether the party is sincere and whether they truly understand what they're undertaking.

Presence: Personal connection. Appeal to Vigmund's humanity and his burden as a leader.
Reason: Strategic arguments about survival, renewal, and the practical benefits of the party's success.
Intuition: Reading Vigmund's state of mind. He is tired and carries the weight of his people. Understanding his emotional state helps the party pitch their appeal effectively.

Reading Vigmund's Responses

  • Interest goes up: Vigmund nods slowly. He leans forward slightly. "You understand what you're saying. I can tell." His voice becomes warmer. "Go on."
  • Interest stays flat (no-motivation argument): He frowns slightly. "That's a fair point, I suppose, but it doesn't change my immediate concerns." He glances toward the window, thinking of his town. "Try again."
  • Interest drops (pitfall): His jaw hardens. "That's not what I expected to hear from you." He stands and walks to the window, turning his back to the party. "I'm reconsidering whether helping you is worth the risk to my people."
  • Patience at 1: He checks the time. "I have other business. If you have something else to say, say it quickly. Otherwise, we're done here."

Outcome: Success

If the party succeeds (reaching Interest 5 or making a convincing argument despite lower rolls), Vigmund agrees:

"I will give you maps of the route north. I will give you warnings about the Bone-Fields. I will give you fresh supplies — as much as I can spare without threatening the town. And you will swear two things to me."

"First: if you succeed, word will be sent back. I want to know if the Fimbulwinter can be beaten, or if we're all just buying time before the end."

He pauses. His jaw works for a moment, as if the next words cost him something.

"Second: I have three hundred refugees in this town. Families. Children. Old people who should have died warm in their beds. If you reach Hrafnborg — if Hrafnborg still stands — you will speak for us. You will tell the King, or whoever holds the walls, that Rindgate's people deserve shelter inside those walls. You will advocate for my refugees as if they were your own kin. You will not let them be turned away because some council decided that the walls are already full enough."

He meets the party's eyes.

"I know what I'm asking. Hrafnborg is under siege. Resources are thin. Every mouth you bring inside the walls is a mouth that eats what the defenders need. But my people cannot survive another winter here. Rindgate is dying. If you won't swear to fight for them at the council table, then I'll give you the maps and the warnings for free — but the supplies stay here. My people need them more than you do."

This goes further than Rickety Frets's witness-oath. The party is not merely swearing to carry a message — they are swearing to advocate for a political cause. When they reach Hrafnborg (Act 3), they will need to spend political capital, argue at councils, and potentially alienate other factions to honor this oath.

📝 Director Note: The Advocacy Oath's Cost

Vigmund's second demand creates a genuine tension that pays off in Act 3:

  • At Hrafnborg, resources are scarce. The city is under siege. Every refugee admitted means less food for defenders.
  • Other factions (the military council, local merchants, potential dwarven allies) may oppose admitting Rindgate's refugees.
  • The party must choose: honor the oath and spend their political influence on refugees, or break the oath and preserve their standing with Hrafnborg's power structure.

If the party accepts and later advocates successfully, they gain significant Word (reputation as people who keep promises under pressure). If they accept and fail to advocate — or never try — they lose 1 Word permanently and Vigmund's name becomes a weight rather than a credential.

The supplies Vigmund withholds if the party refuses the second oath are substantial: 5 days' rations for 4 people, cold-weather gear patches, and a sealed medicine kit. Refusing costs the party real resources heading into the Bone-Fields.

If they accept both oaths, they gain supplies and Vigmund's full support, but they add another obligation to their growing list — and this one has teeth. The Wyrd will hold them accountable.

Part 6: The Departure

Leaving Rindgate

The party rests, resupplies, gathers intelligence, and prepares for the next leg of the journey.

Vigmund walks them to the gates (if friendly) or escorts them out (if they've caused trouble). Either way, there is a moment of final words:

"The Bone-Fields are directly northeast of here. Hard country. The dead walk there freely. Go during daylight hours and keep moving. Don't camp in the Fields if you can help it. The Ivory Tower is on the eastern edge—if you're clever, you can use it as a shelter and a landmark."

(This is foreshadowing Beat 15. Vigmund is unaware the party will visit the tower, but he is giving practical advice.)

Then: "And whatever you're running from or running toward, I hope you find it. This town could use a sign that the world isn't doomed."

The party leaves Rindgate heading northeast toward the Bone-Fields.

GM Notes

📝 Pacing and Atmosphere

This beat can be quick (30 minutes to an hour of table time) if the party moves through it efficiently, or extended (full session) if they rest and investigate thoroughly. Shift the tone from "exploration and wonder" (caves, Rickety Frets) to "military tension and frontier hardship." Rindgate should feel like a town under siege, holding a line against overwhelming pressure.

📝 Character and Campaign Notes

  • Kaelen's growth: She is learning to sense the Wyrd directly, beyond what the texts taught her. This is preparation for her eventual role as a living anchor.
  • Resource pressure: Heat is becoming a serious concern. The party is many days north of warm places. The Steam-Oasis (Beat 17) is still 3+ days away. Every Heat point matters now.
  • Grafvitnir Pressure: This is a good moment to remind the party that they are being hunted. The network is following them. The Pale Maw is ahead of them, preparing defenses. Every settlement they pass through puts them at risk of being identified and intercepted.
  • The lakes: These are creepy and unexplained. The party should feel that something is wrong but not be certain what. This is good. Uncertainty creates tension.
  • The oath to Vigmund: This is optional, but it reinforces the theme that the party's actions matter, that their journey is being witnessed, and that they carry the hopes of people left behind.

Overview

The Bone-Fields are an ancient battlefield where an enormous conflict took place centuries ago. The dead are so numerous and so old that their bones have calcified into the earth itself, forming a landscape of white and gray.

Here, the Rot is strongest. The boundary between Helheim (the realm of the dead) and Midgard (the world of the living) is paper-thin. Draugr do not rise here because they are commanded or because corpses are disturbed—they rise directly from the ground, pulled up by forces that predate even Grafvitnir's current work.

The party must cross the Bone-Fields. It is a gauntlet of:

  1. Environmental hazard (the terrain itself is hostile)
  2. Undead combat (Barrow-Draugr and a Bone-Singer boss)
  3. Spiritual pressure (the presence of death is overwhelming)

By the end of this beat, the party has:

  • Reached level 4 (if they haven't already)
  • Suffered casualties or exhaustion
  • Witnessed the Rot's depth and intelligence
  • Understood that they are running a race against time—Grafvitnir is aware of them and is moving pieces into position

Part 1: The Approach to the Bone-Fields

The Transition Zone

The party is traveling northeast from Rindgate toward the Bone-Fields. The terrain gradually shifts:

  • The grass dies. The scrub becomes sparser. The snow becomes whiter, more sterile.
  • The wind carries a different smell—not the smell of decay (the cold preserves), but the smell of age, of something very old being exposed.
  • Lew becomes nervous. He has avoided this place for years. He tries to mask it, but his unease is visible.

The party reaches the edge of the Bone-Fields in late afternoon. The sun is low and pale. If they rest now, they will camp on the edge and enter at dawn. If they push forward, they enter in the dying light.

📝 GM Decision

This is a good dramatic choice point. Let the party decide whether to camp and face the Fields rested, or push forward and enter in reduced visibility.

The Entering Scene

The Bone-Fields begin without warning.

One moment, you are walking on snow-covered ground. The next, you are stepping onto a landscape of white. Not pristine white, but the white of calcified bone—ancient, brittle, jutting from the frozen ground in irregular formations.

It is as if you have walked into the skeleton of a dead god.

The bones are enormous. Ribs the size of logs. Vertebrae as large as wagon wheels. Skulls that could fit a house inside them. But mixed with the massive bones are countless smaller ones—the remains of soldiers, horses, creatures that fell in whatever battle created this place centuries ago.

The wind here is different. It carries the sound of bone grinding against bone, a creaking that might be wind or might be something far older waking.

The sky above is pale gray. Clouds are moving in from the west. Storm coming. Maybe tonight, maybe tomorrow.

And ahead: the landscape rises in a gentle incline, covered in a white field that extends to the horizon. It is the most desolate place any of you have ever seen.

Kaelen, beside you, is pale. Her hand is on her chest, over her heart. She whispers something—a prayer, or a warning, in a language you don't understand.

"The boundary is so thin here," she says quietly. "I can feel it. The dead are not just beneath us. They're not just in the ground. They're... reaching. Trying to find the way back."

Environmental Hazard: Hard Montage Test

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 2 VP on success, 1 VP partial, 0 VP failure (2 rounds)

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test | Difficulty: Hard | VP Award: 2 VP on success, 1 VP partial, 0 VP failure (2 rounds)

Crossing the Bone-Fields is not simple combat. It is a Hard Montage Test (2 rounds) representing the difficulty of moving through a landscape that is actively hostile:

Difficulty: Hard (adjusted for 4 heroes)
Rounds: 2 (each hero acts once per round; 8 total individual tests)
Success Limit: 5
Failure Limit: 3

Round 1 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate the bone maze Agility or Reason Move carefully through jagged bone formations without cutting feet or breaking ankles. Read patterns in the bones. Are they natural arrangement, or is there intent behind them?
Resist the oppression Presence The presence of death is concentrated and heavy here. The spiritual weight presses down like a hand on the chest. Resist despair. Keep moving. Keep breathing.
Sense the stirring beneath Intuition The ground shifts subtly. Bones settle and creak. Something below is moving, waking, remembering hunger. Maintain awareness. Sound the alarm if needed.

Round 1 Interlude: The Awakening

The ground cracks beneath your feet.

Not everywhere—in scattered places around the party, the calcified earth fissures. The bones beneath shift, moving upward. The ice cracks. And in those cracks, you glimpse movement—not random, but directed.

Then you see it. From one of the cracks, a figure pulls itself up. Skeletal, wrapped in tattered leather and rusted metal. An eye socket glows with sickly pale light.

A Barrow-Draugr. Ancient. Wrong.

It drags itself completely out of the earth and its jaw opens. No sound comes out, but you feel the force of its silence—a scream from a body that has no air to carry the sound.

Around it, more cracks appear. More rising. More of them, at least 6 or 8, emerging from different points in the ground.

And then, from the center of the white bone-field to the north, you hear it: a wail that does carry sound. A voice like wind through a tomb, like the last breath of something dying stretched out infinitely.

A Bone-Singer. One of the dead-kin that has absorbed so many corpses and so much undeath that it has become something more.

It's calling the Barrow-Draugr to attention. Calling them to feed.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Endure the cold and exhaustion Might or Presence Push through the numbing cold. Each step feels like walking on graves. The oppression is physical now, not just spiritual. Keep moving. Keep strength.
Understand the Bone-Singer's power Reason or Intuition This is not natural draugr. This is something older, more powerful. It is binding the dead into a unified will. Recognize that killing it might break the binding.
Keep the group from breaking Presence Fear spreads. Despair is spreading. The party is on the edge of panic. Keep them focused. Keep them together. Keep them alive.

Round 2 Interlude: The Terrible Silence

When the combat ends, there is a profound silence.

The wind dies. The creaking of bones stops. The wailing is gone. It's as if the Fields themselves have exhaled and are now waiting.

The party is in the center of the ancient battlefield. Around them, the bones of the Barrow-Draugr have collapsed back into the earth, twitching occasionally but no longer animate. The Bone-Singer's corpse—or what remains of it—is a twisted thing, more shadow than substance.

To the north, still visible in the distance: a rise in the bone-field, and on that rise, the faint outline of a structure.

The Ivory Tower.

And beyond that, the path north. One more day's journey to the interior highlands. And then, Surt's Forge and the Emberwell.

Bone-Fields Crossing Outcomes

Result Outcome
Hard Success (5+ successes, 0 failures) The party crosses in excellent time (less than a full day's march). They navigate expertly, avoid triggering the Barrow-Draugr entirely. The draugr sense them but do not rise. The spiritual oppression is minimal. They reach the far side in good condition, sighted on the Ivory Tower.
